Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                              Ship [ship]. -noun. [Germanic noun skipa- of obscure origins,
                              possibly meaning “tree hollowed out”]. 1. A vessel of consider-
                              able size, adapted to astrogation. 2. Archaic (or secondary). A
                              vessel adapted to maritime navigation.


                              Na [na] NA. [Origin unchanged from the original Old High Vilani
                              na, meaning pot or urn]. 1. A transportation unit intended to
                              enclose a cargo or payload while protecting it from the environ-
                              ment. The usage includes any mode of transportation: land,
                              sea, air, or space. 2. A starship. 3. A container; an object to
                              hold or transport something.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                       Star Systems
                                  Explorers and space travellers move from system to star system in pursuit of
                              their own personal goals. Star systems may have many different worlds in many
                              different locations or combinations of locations. Star system maps provide general
                              detail of where worlds are in relation to each other and to their central stars.

                    Star systems contain a central star and some number of additional stars and worlds. Star systems which have been cre-
                ated by Traveller Star System Generation can be mapped to the set of star system diagrams in this chapter.

                USING THE MAPS                                                            THE INNER SYSTEM
                    The Star System Maps serve several purposes:                               The Inner System shows the first six orbits (numbered
                    They assist in calculating travel times, Space Ranges,                0 through 5) in a star system. Orbit 6, the first orbit of the
                and opportunities for communication.                                      Outer System is shown for reference.
                    They support the use of sensors and weapons.                               If an orbit contains a companion star, it may have plan-
                    They support visualization of situations for the players              ets in the suborbits shown.
                and the referee.

                             When necessary, star-world distances can be varied deliberately, or with ux. In the rare instance where two adjacent
                             orbits coincide (as in O=9 plus 0.5 and O=10 minus 0.5), the result is a special case.
                             The Decimal Orbital Distances table shows values to a tenth of an orbit; if necessary, values can be calculated to a hun-
                             dredth of an orbit or greater (but observe the MOARN principle: Map Only As Really Necessary).

                 TYPICAL STARPORT CLASS A
                      A Typical Populated World Starport
                           Many good quality services for main trade route business, plus support for ships travelling to the subsector’s back-
                 waters, and base support for the Navy and Scout Service.
                           A. Premium Landing Bays. Elevator platforms retract to the subsurface level. Sheltered access; includes protec-
                 tion from the elements; priority resupply.
                           B. Safe Bays. Elevator platforms retract to the subsurface level. As with Premium Landing Bays, Safe Bays have
                 sheltered access; protection from the elements; priority resupply. There are some ship size restrictions.
                           C. Control Complex. The space traf c control complex includes the personnel and computers that manage the
                 arrival and departure of ships.
                           D. The Cheap Seats. Inexpensive ship parking or storage. Ships are exposed to the elements; access requires
                 walking or driving across the tarmac to a ship.
                           F. Perimeter Fence. Protective barriers limited access to the starport. The fence is both a security barrier limiting
                 access to unauthorized personnel, and a safety barrier preventing access by animals or locals.
                           H. Travellers’ Aid Society Hotel and Complex. Accommodations for travelers and ship crew.
                           L. The Lone Star. A popular meeting place for ship crews (and for gawking tourists and wannabees).
                           M. SDE Starport Defense Establishment. Note the defensive anti-ship missile launchers.
                           N. Naval Base. Naval administration and of ces.
                           S. Scout Base. Scout Service administration and of ces.
                           T. Startown (outside the starport perimeter). The local community which supports the starport labor force. Many
                 businesses in Startown supply goods and services for travelers and ship crew.
                           Y. Landing Strip. Supports winged ships requiring an Air Strip.

                       Not Shown. Underground access to Premium and Safe Bays and the vast subsurface concourse of services available.
                       Not all starports will have all of the facilities shown here.


                                                                             - 22 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                              Starports
                              Every adventure begins and ends at a starport.

                      Starports are the central crossroads through which all interstellar traffic must pass. It stands to reason, then, that interstel-
                lar travellers naturally gravitate to starports... to meet starships and crew, to buy and sell cargoes, and to begin and end their
                adventures.
                      Starports offer at least two opportunities for adventure. Travellers can board a ship and travel to the next world, or they
                can follow the concourse to the main gate and move out to explore the world they are on. In both cases, there is an infinity of
                opportunities for adventure.
                      Beginning Adventures. An adventure can start anywhere, but for convenience and for continuity, some starting point
                must be assigned. That assignment says that an adventure begins at a starport. When characters enter a starport, their intent
                is to find adventure. The details of that adventure may not become clear until later, but it clearly begins at the starport.
                      Ending Adventures. Likewise, the details, the climax and even the payoff for adventures may take place anywhere, but
                every adventurer knows in his or her or its heart that it’s not over until they reach the starport.
                      The Endless Cycle. At the very moment that an adventure ends, a new one begins. The endless cycle in Traveller is
                the end of an old adventure and the beginning of a new one; each builds on the previous, and the cycle never ends until the
                characters stop going to the starport.

                   A world with a starport has a surface Downport.                   and shuttled down. Some worlds are naturally inhospitable

                 A TYPICAL STARPORT CLASS E
                 A Typical Frontier Starport
                   No facilities beyond a broad at expanse of exposed bedrock capable of supporting a starship landing peds.
                   The sheltered bay in the background allows ships to refuel (and water landings).
                   Note the trading company sheds providing temporary shelter and security for cargo.


                                                                          - 29 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                About Starships
                                  Starships carry passengers and goods between star systems; they are the
                              instruments which allow interstellar governments to project their power and to pro-
                              tect their citizens; they are the means by which companies, people, and govern-
                              ments explore and exploit the universe.

                    A starship is a complex assembly of many technologies: life support, drives, power systems, sensors, and (because the
                universe can be dangerous) weapons and defenses.

                An Overview of Starships                                            Jump, which carries the ship a parsec or more in about a
                     Many descriptions of starships (or spacecraft) are con-        week. Slower systems (Not As Fast As Light) are possible,
                tent with a few words about interstellar speed or overall di-       as are a variety of rarer higher performance drives.
                mensions. Role-playing adventures with starships will ulti-              Power Systems. The ship has a Power system which
                mately address every aspect of a starship: there will come a        provides energy for operations and for the drives. The most
                time when someone needs to know the size of the crew, or            common is the starship power plant which uses commonly
                the payload, how fast the ship can travel from the MainWorld        available hydrogen as fuel. Other options include fission sys-
                to some satellite, or hundreds of other details.                    tems or exotic energy systems.
                     The comprehensive Traveller starship design and con-                Sensors. The ship has an array of sensors capable of
                struction rules provide that detail, whether before adventures      detecting other ships, potential dangers, and basic informa-
                ever begin, or later as needed.                                     tion. The sensor array includes communicators for interac-
                     A Starship is a ship capable of travel between the stars.      tions with other ships, other worlds, and traffic control.
                A ship restricted to a single star system is a spaceship, or             Control Systems. The ship has a variety of control
                simply a ship. Spacecraft smaller than 100 tons are called          systems which monitor the ships mechanisms and allow the
                small craft, or boats.                                              crew to adjust or direct their operation. Some activity may be
                                                                                    directed from the command center (the Bridge) and some
                WHAT’S IN A STARSHIP?                                               are co-located with the mechanisms themselves.

                Travel Between Star Systems
                     Most ships are expected to move between star systems            Embrace Some Mission and Properly Perform It
                and include capabilities for Jump or some other interstellar              Every ship is built to accomplish some specific function;
                drive.                                                               in addition to all of the above, the ship may carry equipment
                     The standard interstellar movement system is Jump,              intended to execute that mission.
                which moves a ship a parsec (or single digit multiples of a
                parsec) in about a week.                                             THERE’S MORE!

                              - 32 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                            Adventure Class Ships
                                  Starships for characters and for role-playing have specific requirements in
                              terms of size, affordability, and crew requirements. Adventure Class Ships are
                              perfect for most character activities: characters can (alone, or with companions, or
                              with hired help) acquire, operate, and benefit from ships of this size.

                     Broadly speaking, starships are divided into three types based roughly on size: Adventure Class Ships (with tonnage
                between 100 and 2400 tons), Battle Class Ships (2500 tons upward to about 100,000 tons) and Fleet Class Ships (the larg-
                est of ships at hundreds of thousands of tons).
                     Of the three classes, the crew and support requirements preclude the two larger classes from most player interactions.
                When characters do encounter them, they are usually background, or derelicts to be explored.

                Challenges, and Rewards                                                     That process includes great planning and preparation:
                      Adventure Class Ships provide a variety of challenges             characters need to decide what model is best suited for their
                to characters. Although they provide transportation, they re-           needs, what accessories, what capabilities, what options.
                quire skill (and a bit of experience) to operate them. They             Then they must balance benefit with cost, and with available
                cost money--for fuel, repairs, upkeep, crew salaries, and               funds.
                even life support--that has to come from somewhere, and                     Alternatives To A Ship. It is possible to have great
                usually requires devoting some time to fund-raising.                    adventures without a ship. An ATV (tracked, wheeled, or air
                      They provide an opportunity for adventure: a way to               cushion) can support explorations on a wilderness world in
                visit worlds off the well-travelled spacelanes, or beyond the           search of abandoned military bases, or the ruins of some
                borders of safe territory. They provide a safe base of op-              forgotten civilization. A small craft, fitted out with life support
                erations: with resources, defenses, and protection. They are            and sensors, can carry several characters to the remote
                ultimately the means by which characters achieve their high-            reaches of a star system in search of derelict ships, or ma-
                est goal: travel.                                                       rooned explorers.
                                                                                            But ultimately, characters want a ship.

                 Levels of Starship Design
                   Broadly speaking, starships are classi ed based on size and usage.
                                                              The range of transportation units operating in land, sea, air, and near-world
                          VVehicles                        vacuum environments. Vehicles are the means for player characters to travel on
                                                           and near worlds.
                                                              Space vessels ranging on tonnage from about 10 tons to just under 100 tons.
                      SC   Small Craft                     They support interactions with other ships, and with world surfaces.
                                                              Often included in the Adventure Class Ship category.
                                                              Ships with tonnage between 100 (the minimum possible) and 2400 tons.
                  ACS      Adventure Class Ships Adventure class ships are appropriate for ownership by, and operation by, player
                                                           characters.
                                                              Ships from 2500 tons upward to about 100,000 tons. Battle class ships may
                  BCS      Battle Class Ships              be encountered, but are rarely under the control of player characters. They are
                                                           important for strategic decisions in naval warfare.
                                                              The ultimate in starships: the largest of ships with tonnages in hundreds of
                  FCS      Fleet Class Ships               thousands of tons (to the practical maximum of about a million tons).
                                                              Fleet class ships are most important in strategic government operations.

                the wide range of technologies available: for drives, weap-
                ons, sensors,and other details.
                       But designing ships takes time, and so a dozen or so
                pre-designed ships are considered standard and commonly
                available. They are described here in enough detail that any
                character who finds, or buys, or borrows, or discovers one
                can immediately put it to use.
                       But these ships are only half-written designs (deliber-
                ately so). The descriptions provide drives and hull sizes, but
                still leave accommodations and cargo tonnages and arma-
                ments to be specified by the ultimate user.
                       On the other hand, three of the ships (the Scout, the
                Free Trader, and the Patrol Frigate) are more fully described
                with fully detailed ShipSheets, and a footnoted description
                pointing out details and features that take advantage of the
                ship creation system, and noting the nuances of weapons,
                defenses, and sensors.
                                                                                      Type BC Battle Cruiser                        1200
                                                                                        1200 tons TL-14                            BC-MS44

                THE BATTLECRUISER                                                            Passengers 24 (Ship’s Troops)
                     And yet some Adventure Class Ships are best seen as                           Cargo
                adversaries: opponents or foils for the ship that the charac-            Acceleration G= 4 +1G in Atm 2+
                ters do operate. The BattleCruiser is an example of an adver-                   Jump J= 4
                sary, shown here with partial details, and also as a footnoted             Jump Flash= Standard
                description with more insights into its use and operation.

                     Various organizations produce detailed recognition guides. Within the Imperium, there are five commonly known starship
                recognition guides:
                     The Trader’s Guild Catalog Of Ships provides basic information about most commercial ships operating within the Im-
                perium. Its naval forces supplement is sketchier and details basic information about armed ships in the region.
                     The Imperial Navy’s SRDB Ship Recognition Data Base is an encrypted data base identifying all of its own forces, most
                commercial shipping, and a fair set of information about neighboring naval forces.
                     The Imperial Interstellar Scout Service’s Known Ships consolidates sensor scans of all ships its survey and exploration
                missions encounter and redistributes it within the service.
                     The AAB Argushiigi Admegulasha Bilanidin Ship List. The Imperial Archive maintains a long-term historical recogni-
                tion guide detailing ships of the First, Second, and Third Imperia as well as ships of allies and enemies.
                     The Travellers’ Aid Society’s Green List notes all ships calling at worlds with TAS facilities; rating ships in a spectrum
                Green-Amber-Red based on dependability, safety, and customer service.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Starship Design and Construction
                                  Starships are designed and constructed for specific missions: naval, explor-
                              atory, trade, research, even leisure. The process of design and construction (the
                              field of naval architecture) carries the ship designer through a series of steps which
                              identify ship component requirements and address them.

                   Ship design is a continuous feedback process: each step seems to mandate changes in what had been decided before.
                Eventually, however, the ship design appears complete and can be finalized with a ship name.

                ACS ADVENTURE CLASS SHIPS                                          THE COMPONENTS OF A STARSHIP
                     Adventure Class ships are starships and spacecraft                   A starship consists of a variety of components, each
                suitable for use by groups of player characters. The ships         with its own particular importance and requirements.
                are large enough to carry profitable cargos but small enough              The Hull. The starship hull is the container or enclosure
                that the activities of the individual characters matter.           into which all other components must be fitted. Hulls are de-
                     Tonnages. Adventure Class Ships are built using stan-         fined by a Size (in tons), a Structure, and a Configuration
                dard hulls between 100 tons and 2400 ton displacement.             (shape and streamlining).
                     Vessels smaller than 100 tons are Small Craft. Ships                 Armor. Ships may be equipped with armor to protect
                larger than 2400 tons are BCS Battle Class Ships. Ships and        them against attack and from hostile environments.
                Small Craft use this ACS Design System; Battle Class Ships                Drives. Ships may be equipped with a variety of drives
                use a different design system.                                     providing power and the ability to move both between plan-
                     Drag And Drop Components. The components for                  ets and between star systems.
                ACS ships are standardized for selection from tables (as                  Sensors. A ship has a set of technological eyes and
                opposed to being created by formulas or percentages as in          ears for exploring systems and detecting other ships.
                BCS ships).                                                               Weapons. A ship may be armed for its own protection
                     Tech Level Adjustments. The standard components               and to accomplish its mission.
                can be adjusted (in tonnage, range, efficiency) based on                  Defenses. A ship may be equipped with a variety of de-
                available Tech Level.                                              fensive capabilities.

                by consulting the Drive Potential Table. A Jump Drive-A in
                                                                                    an Anti-Layer imposes no additional cost or tonnage.
                Hull-A requires a Power Plant-A (or a Power Plant with Po-
                                                                                         In some cases, an Anti-Layer confers no additional pro-
                tential= 2, which is the same thing).

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                (since Plate already provides x10 against Blast). On the oth-
                er hand, Anti-Blast confers an improvement on Shell (which
                ordinarily has x5 against Blast).

                Special Armor Variations
                     Several armor variations are possible.
                     Self Healing Armor. Polymer is Self-Healing; it repairs
                damage equal to its TL every turn. Organic is Slow Self-
                Healing; it regenerates damage equal to its TL every day.
                     Regenerating Armor. Organic is Regenerating. A layer
                of destroyed Regenerating Armor is restored after one week.

                Armor Coatings
                     Armor, in addition to layers, can be coated for additional
                protection. If a destructive attack is ultimately successful on
                a hit location which has a coating, the coating effects are
                eliminated for the remainder of the battle (and until repaired).
                                                                                    FISSION POWER PLANT
                     Reflec. A mirror-bright reflective coating provides pro-
                tection against Burn or Heat.

                heat and burns off, thus protecting the Layer below. Ablat          things) to provide the energy required.

                10 STARSHIP DRIVES                                                  dividual mechanisms switch to emergency power and can
                     Starships require drives to move and power systems to          continue in operation for a short period of time.
                provide them energy.                                                     Decentralized Power. The ship’s primary power source
                     Drives are divided into three categories:                      supports its interplanetary and interstellar drives. Individual
                     Interplanetary Drives or InSystem Drives are capable           mechanisms (sensors, most weapons, control consoles) are
                of moving a ship between worlds in a star system. They in-
                clude Maneuver Drives and Gravitic Drives. Lifters (installed
                as a Hull Fitting) are a very low power Interplanetary Drive.
                     Interstellar Drives capable of moving a ship between
                star systems. The Jump Drive is the principal system be-
                cause it can propel a ship in multiples of parsecs in weeks.
                The NAFAL Not As Fast As Light Drive is capable of signifi-
                cant fractions of light speed, but still requires years to move
                between star systems.
                     Power Systems capable of providing the energy that
                the drives need. The Power Plant is a fusion power system
                using Hydrogen as fuel. The High Tech Anti-Matter Plant is
                vastly more efficient. The exotic Collector requires greater
                time to recharge but is independent of specific fuels.

                                                                                                  Types of Maneuver Drives

                                                                                             M                G                 Z
                                                                                          Maneuver          Gravitic          Lifters
                                                                                           1000D             10D                1D

                                                                                 M MANEUVER DRIVE
                                                                                      The Maneuver Drive M-Drive is a powerful drive system
                                                                                 that interacts with gravity fields to produce thrust.
                                                                                      The M-Drive is installed as a drive. It requires a Power
                                                                                 Plant with potential equal to or greater than the M-Drive. For
                                                                                 example, a ship with Maneuver-5 requires a Power Plant
                                                                                 with Potential-5, a Fission Plant with Potential-5, or an AM
                                                                                 Plant with Potential-5. It cannot use a Collector.
                                                                                      The Maneuver Drive operates efficiently within 1000 D
                                                                                 of a gravity source. Beyond that limit, its efficiency drops to
                                                                                 about 1%.
                                                                                      Performance. M-Drive performance is evaluated in G
                 JUMP DRIVE                                                      (Drive Potential = Gs).

                      Comfort 5 or greater insulates against tension and San-
                ity checks, but may be too expensive. Comfort 4 produces
                a problem on Flux= -5, or about once a month. Comfort 3
                produces a problem on Flux= -4, or about once every two
                weeks.
                      Daily. Most ships establish a routine: the daily Comfort
                roll is done at the end of the Day Watch (16 hours into the
                day; as most crew end their day and the sleeping period be-
                gins). Any Sanity Checks happen through the night.


                27 THE QUICK SHIP PROFILE
                     The QSP provides a basic identification of the capabili-
                ties of a ship.
                     The Quick Ship Profile shows the Ship Mission, Hull
                Tonnage and Configuration, Maneuver Acceleration in Gs,
                and Jump ability.
                      It can be expanded with two extensions:
                     The Vehicle Extension. The Vehicle Extension Vx is a
                variable length string recounting the vehicles and small craft
                carried by the ship.
                     The Crew Extension. The Crew Extension Cx is a vari-
                able length string detailing the crew members for the ship.




                 STARSHIP ANNUAL MAINTENANCE
                   Ships require an annual Complete Overhaul (at a cost of
                 0.1% (1/1000th of the cash price of the ship; it requires two
                 weeks at a class A or B starport.
                   Crew members generally take their vacations at this time,
                 but continue to draw salary.

                                                                                   P = (EP / Hull) *2
                      Drive Potential determines drive performance.                                                            Drive EP
                      Jump Drive with Potential-2 produces Jump-2;                                                              A    100
                      Maneuver Drive with Potential-4 produces 4G.                                                              B    200
                      Drive Potential can be calculated based on Drive En-             P= Potential                             C    300
                 ergy Points and Hull Tonnage. P= (EP / Hull)*2                        EP= Energy Points                        D    400
                      Jump Drive-A has 100 Energy Points. Installed in a               Hull= Hull Tons                          E    500
                 100-ton hull, the drive produces ((100/100)*2=) Potential-2.          Maximum 9. Round Down.                   F    600
                      Round Down. Drive Potential rounds down; the slight-                                                      G    700
                                                                                     Drive Potential is the expected perfor-
                 est deficit reduces Potential to the next lower whole number.                                                  H    800
                                                                                   mance of a Drive (of any type) based on
                      Precise Calculations Are Possible. Because hulls             the EP Energy Points of the Drive and the    J    900
                 can be overtonned or undertonned, Potential calculation           tonnage of the Hull.                         K 1000
                 can use hull tonnages other than full multiples of 100 tons.                                                   L 1100

                 DRIVE NEXI
                       Drives can be ganged and their combined output har-
                 nessed using Nexi.
                       A Nexus (plural = Nexi) connects the output up to 9
                 identical mechanisms, allowing the joined devices to pro-
                 duce greater output.
                       For example, an N2 or NN G-Drive consists of two N
                 G-Drives connected by a Nexus. The nexus is basically a
                 connection; it adds no tonnage or cost.
                       A Nexus can create other drives outside of, or beyond
                 the standard ones shown on the table: N3, K4, Z9. An A9
                 drive (consisting of nine Drive-A and a Nexus) outputs 900
                 EP, is (9 x 2=) 18 tons, and costs MCr9.
                       Combined drives can themselves be combined (desig-
                 nated by a prefix digit): 4A4 indicates (4 x 4 = ) 16 Drive-A.
                 The largest drive available is 9Z9 (= 9 x 9 = ) 81 Drive-Z.
                       Any Drive-Z has 2400 EP. The Z9 has nine times that,
                 or 21,600 EP. The 9Z9 has nine times that, or 194,400 EP.
                       Drive 9Z9 in a 190,000 ton Hull produces Potential-1.
                 SBecause 9Z9 is the largest ganged drive possible, a larger
                 Hull requires at least two drives to achieve Potential-1.
                       For example, Jump Drive-A has 100 EP. Two Drive-A
                 connected with a Nexus becomes a Drive-A2 with 200 EP.
                 In a 400-ton Hull, this drive produces (200/400*2= ) Poten-
                 tial-1: enough to produce Jump-1.
                       Jump Drive-B is 15 tons; Jump Drive-A2 is 20 tons,
                 which is clearly not the optimum configuration. Such choic-
                 es are sometimes made to use surplus, or cheaply available
                 mechanisms.
                       Theoretically, Drive Nexi can be used to combine any
                 same-type drives, including Rockets, Thrusters, HEPlaR,
                 and even Orion Drives.

                                                                       Starships are created to accomplish missions. The descriptive
                                Starship
                 02             Missions
                                                                    terms for missions have speci c meanings, but they are de ned to
                                                                    allow broad interpretation and substantial overlap.
                                                                       Select a mission for the ship (or assign it after the ship is designed)
                                                                       State the mission as a one-, two- or (rarely) three- letter code.
                                                                       Multiple identical letter codes (AA, AAA) may use a digit (A2, A3)
                  Apply Mission Modi ers after the Mission as needed in the format EFF. Although the technically correct usage states
                Mission rst, the converse has become commonplace as FFE. and is tolerated: Modi er-Modi er-Mission (SDB= System
                Defense-Boat). Actual meanings are subject to common sense, and may be ambiguous.

                                 Hull
                 04              Tons and Costs
                                                                            Hull Size (in tons) has a cost determined by tonnage and configura-
                                                                         tion. It is possible to create a ship with more than one hull.

                                                                         CREATING A HULL (AB)
                                                                          A. Select a Hull Size to determine volume (and payload).
                                                                          B. Select Component Hulls (Pods, Barges, or Subhulls).

                      Configuration determines ship appearance and performance.

                     C. Select a Configuration and note ship performance charac-

                  C    Cluster            x2   -5      --    1      -3      No          An accumulation of compartments.

                  P    Planetoid          x1   -2      --    9      -1       ?          A hollowed nickel-iron asteroid.
                  U    Unstreamlined /2        -1      --    9        0    Yes          An enclosure whose protrusions increase drag.
                  S    Streamlined        /3     0     --    9      +1     Yes          An enclosure with cowlings and fairings to decrease drag.
                  A    Airframe           /4   +1      +1    9      +2     Yes          A winged enclosure for better performance in atmosphere
                  L    Lifting Body       /5   +1      +1    9      +3     Yes          A radically streamlined lifting-surface body

                  Starship Construction

                                 Starship
                 06              Hull Fittings
                                                                                  Hull Structure determines the ship’s construction technique
                                                                              and its base armor. Fittings enhance ship performance.

                                                                              CREATING A HULL (DE)
                                                                                  D. Select a Hull Structure to define the basic nature and
                                                                              construction technique of the Hull.
                                                                                  E. Add Fittings to allow for landing gear, flotation and sub-
                                                                              mergence capability, and wings.



                D HULL STRUCTURE
                       Type- - - - - Con guratIon - - - - -     TL= AV=               Comment                                           Features
                 A      Plate C B         - U S A           -     8      8 =TL        Standard/ default.            Also called Frame and Plate
                 S      Shell -       -   -        S A L          9      5 =TL /2 Frameless alloy skin.                   Lighter (Half Tonnage)
                 P Polymer C B            - U S A           -    10      5 =TL /2 Specialized plastics.                             Self-Healing
                 F       FeN -        - P U        -      - -    11     20            Hollowed nickel-iron asteroid.            Upgradable to U
                 O Organic C B                U S A         -    12      6 =TL /2 Captured or farmed organisms.                    Regenerating
                 C Charged C B                U S                14 28* =TLx2 Powered improvement of Plate *AV= TLx2 when powered
                   Hull Structure is a naval architecture decision (the default is Plate).
                   Structure does not usually affect Hull tonnage, but Shell Hull tonnage is halved.
                   Structure does not usually affect Hull cost; but Organic Hull cost is halved; Charged Hull cost adds MCr1 per 100 tons.

                CREATING A HULL (FGH)
                    Jump Drives require a Jump Readiness option.
                    F. Jump Field Type. Choose a Jump Field option.
                    G. Safe Jump Distance. Calculate the distance.
                    H. Interference. Note formula to determine if Initiation Inter-
                                                                                                    Jump Fields                      07
                ference occurs.

                JUMP BUBBLE                                                       JUMP GRID
                     A Jump Bubble creates a spherical field around the                A closely-conforming Jump Grid channels jump energy
                ship and centered on the jump drive. Jump Bubble is the           through a mesh of conduits and cables embedded in the hull.
                standard for generating a Jump Field; it does not interfere            Jump Grid allows a reduced safe jump distance, making
                with armor and produces a standard jump flash.                    it possible for a ship to jump closer to a gravity source. On
                     Jump Bubble allows a ship to vary its effective tonnage      the other hand, the Jump Grid reduces the strength of armor
                from mission to mission (which makes Drop Tanks and Vari-         and increases telltale jump flash.
                able Jump Container Ships possible).                                   Difficulties. Damaged Jump Grids are difficult to repair.
                     Difficulties. A Jump Bubble may enclose nearby debris,       (Jump Plates are bolted on to repair gaps in grid coverage,
                which may result in a Jump Mishap.                                which converts it to Jump Plates).

                JUMP PLATES (ALSO REPAIR PLATES)
                     The original system for creating a Jump Field consisted of Bolt-On Jump Plates (one plate per 10 tons of Hull; bolted on
                the surface in an evenly spaced pattern. Jump Plates are required for any Experimental, Prototype, or Early Tech Jump Drive
                (or Experimental, Prototype, or Early Hop, Skip, or Higher Order drive).
                     Jump Plates reduce armor value for the outer armor layer. They produce higher values of D.
                     Repair Plates. Jump Plates are used to repair damaged Jump Grids: they are bolted on damaged hull to restore the Jump
                Grid continuity. When in use, Repair Plates effectively convert Jump Grid to Jump Plates for Jump Field production.
                     The Hull Armor at the repaired Compartment of Hit Location is reduced by half.
                     Advantages. Jump Plates are a lower tech component of the Jump Drive. Jump Plates are a repair option and can be
                bolted over damage hull Jump Grids (reducing the overall to Plates).

                 F JUMP FIELD TYPES
                 Type Strength Comment                  Armor Mod Flash
                 Bubble    100    Default                  std      std Bubble produces the standard value for D (Table 07G).
                 Grid       80    Embedded in the Hull     Mod -1D +1 Grid reduces safe jump distance D.
                 Plates    140    1 Plate Per 10 Hull Tons Mod /2   +1 Plates produce the greatest value for D.
                 Flash: Change in Jump Flash Size from Standard.


                 G CALCULATE SAFE JUMP DISTANCE                              H INITIATION INTERFERENCE
                 The safe jump distance D for a speci c ship is              The safe distance to avoid initiation interference is

                 D = S /E -K                                                 X = S /E -(D +K) (+Flux)
                 D= Diameters (from the Gravity Source Center).              X= Initiation Interference. If X < 1, no jump interference.
                 S= Jump Field Strength (Chart 07F).                                Flux is optional, but if rolled, must be used.
                 E= Drive Ef ciency (from Chart 10X).                        D= Diameters (from the Gravity Source Center).
                     =1 unless Drive Tech Stage Effects are in place.        S= Jump Field Strength (Chart 07F).
                 K= Engineer Skill and Jump Drive Knowledge.                 E= Drive Ef ciency (from Chart 10X).
                                                                                    =1 unless Drive Tech Stage Effects are in place.
                                                                             K= Engineer Skill and Jump Drive Knowledge.
                      For example, Fat Trader March Harrier with Jump             For example, Fat Trader March Harrier (S=140, E=1, K=3),
                 Plates (S= 140), a Standard Jump Drive (E= 1) and           fleeing a security inspection, reaches 100D. X= 140/1 - (100+3) =
                 Engineer Jump Knowledge-3. D= 140 /1 - 3 = 140-3            140 - 103 = +37. X is greater than zero: there will be a misjump if
                 = 137. This ship can safely Jump at 137 Diameters.          the ship attempts Jump (even Flux -5 can’t change X to 0 or less).
                      For example, Gunned Escort Neon with Jump                   For example, Gunned Escort Neon (S=80, E=1.2, K=5)
                 Grid (S= 80), Advanced Jump Drive (E=120%= 1.2)             reaches 70D as it approaches an outpost when it suddenly en-
                 and Engineer Jump Knowledge-5. D= 80 /1.2 - 5 =             counters an overwhelming enemy force. X= (80/1.2) - (70 +5)=
                 66 - 5 = 61. The ship can safely jump at 61 Diameters.      66 - 55 = -11. X is less than 1; the ship can safely jump.

                        Names are assigned to the types of Armor, and those names themselves have TL and TL Stages.
                        For example, TL6 Plate Armor is called Aluminum (AV= 6). It can be further re ned as Experimental Aluminum (TL3 AV=3),
                        or Advanced Aluminum (TL 9 AV=9). For some cases, the protection values of a speci c Armor Type and Name may dupli-
                        cate the values of a different Type and Name. Tech Stage differences in cost and mass are ignored.

                                          Starship
                           10             Drives
                                                                                                                  SELECTING DRIVES
                                                                                                                       A ship may require:
                                                                                                                       An Interplanetary Drive (M, G, R,
                                                                                                                  H) for travel within a star system and to
                                                                                                                  and from world surfaces. Default is M.
                           STARSHIP DRIVES                                                                             An Interstellar Drive (N, J, H, S) to
                                                                                                                  allow travel between star systems. The
                                                                                                                  default is J.
                                                                                                                       A Power System (A, C, U, P) to
            Power
                                                                                                                  provide energy for operations. The de-
                                           8                    8                 19                        14    fault is P. In some cases, an alternative
                                   P                   U                   A                       C              Power Source (B, H, F+) may be se-
                              Power Plant           Fission           Anti-Matter               Collector         lected.
                                                                                                                       W. Tech Level Availability.
                                                                                                                       X. Drive Efficiency
                                                                                                                       Y. Drive Cost.


            Maneuver
                                                                                                                       Z1 and Z2. Drive Potential.

                                  M                   G                    R                       H                   Drives (and Power Systems) pro-

                                                                                                                  11Y) at base TL. Other TLs may pro-
                                                                                                                  duce other Potentials based on Drive
                                                                                                                  Efficiency. Efficiency also affects fuel


            Interstellar
                                                                                                                  consumption (if applicable). Fuel is the
                                                                                                                  multiplier for fuel required (or time re-

                                   B                   H                  F+                                      economy can produce an Experimental
                                Battery             Fuel Cell         Fusion Plus                                 G-Drive.

                     MCr/ton=    2.0       0.5     0.5    1.0    1.0           2.0        3.0     1.0      2.0       0.5     1.5    1.0
                    No drive may be smaller than the Drive-A of the class, even after modi cations like Stage Effects.
                    Example: A TL12 200-ton Hull-B can select a TL12 Jump Drive-D (Table 11Y above) at a cost of 25 tons and MCr25.
                    At TL12, the maximum Jump a standard drive can produce (Table 10W) is Jump-3 based on standard TL. Jump Drive-D
                 in Hull-B can produce (Table 12Z1) Jump-4 (but 10W constrains it to Jump-3). The Hull-B Jump-D combination, assuming a
                 Power Plant with equal potential, requires (Table 13) 3 x 10% x 200 tons = 60 tons of fuel for a Jump-3.
                    A society at TL 16 cannot produce standard Hop Drives. It can produce a Hop-A for installation in a Hull-B as an Experi-
                 mental Hop-2 (at 50% ef ciency, round down to Hop-1) with various tonnage (x3), cost (x10), and fuel (x2) handicaps, and
                 it would require a Jump Field based on Jump Plates. Nevertheless, the result is an improvement over most other options.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                The Elements Of The ShipCard
                                 The ShipCard is a unique record of the design of a starship, and the Combat
                              Card is a companion record used in starship combat.
                    The FillForm is the design form used to record the specifics of a starship design, and is documentation of components
                selected and their performance. The ShipCard is used in space combat to note hits as they are received, and the resulting
                degradation of performance.

                THE SPACE COMBAT PROCESS                                                           THE SHIPCARD
                     Space Combat involves weapons attacks against star-                                The ShipCard records the various components of the
                get ships. A successful attack produces a Hit on the target.                       ship in schematic fashion based on ship and component ton-
                                                                                                   nage and the decisions of the ship designer.
                          Possible hit locations are presented on the                                   Create The ShipCard. Create a ShipCard for each star-
                      ShipCard. The actual location of the Hit is deter-                           ship as needed or appropriate. Keep the ShipCard available
                      mined by Flux, and then applied against a Sub-                               as needed.
                      Compartment based on a 1D roll.                                                   Use The ShipCard. Available ShipCards record the
                                                                                                   results of space combat (or environmental events). The in-
                      Location Destroyed. The SubCompartment is marked                             tention is that the ShipCard supports character role-playing
                as destroyed. It is Out-Of-Action and cannot function.                             by involving individual characters at specific locations during
                     Possibly Damaged Instead. It is possible that Immedi-                         the action. Specific characters lend their personal skills and
                ate Action Damage Control may convert the result to Dam-                           expertise to the operation of the ship and its associated sen-
                aged (and the location may remain operational).                                    sors, weapons, and defenses. As damage is taken, charac-
                     After the battle is over, Destroyed or Damaged Loca-                          ters are subject to possible injury, and can lend their labors
                tions are evaluated and repairs may be attempted.                                  to damage control and repairs.

                     Begin with a blank ShipCard. Use the half-page for ships           T TARGETTING

                   Z     2400       25        100    17         -12   +12               Compartments.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                         How Maneuver Works
                                 Starships and spacecraft move within a star system using one of the several
                              available in-system or interplanetary drives.
                     The activity that carries ships from world surface to orbit and between worlds in a system is collectively called maneuver.
                     Gravity-Based Maneuver Drives. The three commonly available in system drives are Maneuver (M-Drive), Gravitic
                (G-Drive), and Lifters (sometimes Z-Drive). A similar technology is the basis for Not As Fast As Light (N-Drive). Because the
                M-Drive carries the word Maneuver in its title, various references to Maneuver Drives can be confusing: the word may refer
                to a specific type of drive (the Maneuver Drive or M-Drive), or to a general class of drives that propel ships. The meaning can
                usually be derived from context.
                     Non-Gravity-Based Drives. In addition, several non-gravity based In-System drives are available (although not de-
                scribed here) and include: Rocket, HEPlaR, Orion, and Dean.

                HOW MANEUVER WORKS                                                    and worlds, and are essentially unusable beyond those dis-
                   Elementary instruction systems explain:                            tances.
                                                                                           Compensators. Integral to Maneuver Drives, Gravitic
                           Maneuver drives interact with gravity to move              Drives, and Lifters is an inertial compensation component
                      spaceships. Parts of the drive reach out and grab               which counteracts the effects of acceleration on occupants
                      the gravity of a world or a star and push against it            of the ship.
                      to make the ship move. Isn’t that neat?

                     Maneuver Drives Are Gravity-Based. The theoretical               rection involve adding vectors over time. Movement between
                underpinnings of Maneuver involve the strength of gravita-            locations involves acceleration for the initial portion of the
                tional fields from stars and worlds. The practical result is that     route, an optional period of coasting, and deceleration to the

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Rocket Thrust                                                       HEPlaR DRIVE
                      Rocket Drives may apply thrust for maneuver purposes.
                      Thrust takes two forms: micro-thrust and vector change.
                      Micro-Thrust. The drive makes a small adjustment by
                expending one ton of fuel, producing a course change that
                will enter a stable orbit around a world or satellite.
                      Vector Change. The drive expends fuel units equal
                to 10% of its net tonnage (total ship tons minus tonnage of
                empty fuel tanks). The ship changes its vector by 1G.
                      For example, a 100-ton ship with full tanks for 90 tons of
                fuel expends 10 tons of fuel to change its vector 1G. It then
                has a net tonnage of 90 tons: it expends 9 tons of fuel to
                change its vector 1G. It then has a net tonnage of 81 tons: it
                expends 8 tons for fuel to change its vector 1G.
                      A 100-ton ship with full tanks for 90 tons of fuel expends
                60 tons of fuel to change its vector 6G. The same ship with
                its tanks drained to only 25 tons of fuel has a net tonnage of      FUEL CONSUMPTION BY ROCKET/ HEPLAR
                35 tons; it expends (3.5 x 6 =) 21 tons of fuel to change its       Vector      ------ Rockets -------     ------ HEPlaR ------
                vector 6G.                                                          Change Fuel Tons        Ship Tons Fuel Tons Ship Tons
                                                                                    No.        Required Remaining Required Remaining
                HEPlaR                                                                 1          10         90                  1   99
                    HEPlaR High Efficiency Plasma Re-combustion is an                  2            9        81                  1   98
                advanced rocket drive system. A power plant heats hydro-               3            8        73                  1   97
                gen fuel to a near fusion state which is expelled as reaction          4            7        66                  1   96
                mass. The resulting thrust is an order of magnitude greater            5            6.5      59.5                1   95
                than traditional chemical rockets.                                     6            6        53.5                1   94

                Fusion Orion (O2 Orion-2)                                          Pressure Plate, the Detonators, and the Feed Mechanism.
                     The mid-range development of Orion uses a combina-                 The Pressure Plate. The common denominator for
                tion of nuclear dampers, induced fusion, and inertial com-         Orion O-Drives is the massive pressure plate that absorbs
                pensators to produce survivable thrust. Radioactive contam-        the propelling explosive force and protects the ship from the

                    The most advanced incarnation of Orion uses anti-              Pressure Plate is less massive. For O3, the Pressure Plate

                produce survivable thrust. The thrust produces no radiation             The Orion Detonators. Regardless of technology (fu-
                or environmental damage.                                           sion, fission, or AM), Orion detonators are self-contained

                Components of the Orion Drive                                      the system (typically 0.2 kilotons; dialable from 0.01 to 1.0).
                   The Orion Drive is composed of three elements: the              Each is a cylinder 8 cm in diameter and 20 cm long (about 1

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 ORION DRIVE
                                                                                 liter), and designed for use with an automated feed system.
                                                                                 The devices typically have a variety of fail-safe interlocks
                                                                                 and codes, remote tracking and identifications, and other
                                                                                 non-public protections.
                                                                                       The fuel feed mechanisms for Orion hold about 7,000
                                                                                 detonators per ton (whether fission, fusion, or AM).
                                                                                       Fission Orion detonators are small fission bombs acti-
                                                                                 vated by a remote signal.
                                                                                       Fusion Orion detonators are fusible elements which
                                                                                 are externally activated by nuclear damper technology. They
                                                                                 are safe outside of the Orion system.
                                                                                       AM Orion detonators are a small amount of magnetical-
                                                                                 ly-held anti-matter activated by a remote signal.
                                                                                       Orion O-Drive expends 0.001% (= one ten-thousandth)
                                                                                 of net tonnage for Vector Change. For example, a 1,000-ton
                                                                                 hull expends one-tenth of a ton (or about 700 detonators) for
                                                                                 a Vector Change.

                 One Minute                   60                                 propriate acceleration from 1G to 9G. Although acceleration

                 One Month            2,592,000          5,000        370        gravity-based, Orion does not include inertial compensation.

                 One Year            31,536,000         60,900      4,500
                    Expending one detonator (appropriately dialed for yield)     THRUSTERS
                 per second produces the appropriate acceleration from 1G             Thrusters are mechanical devices which convert rotary
                 to 9G (for any tonnage of ship up to 2400 tons).                motion into uni-directional motion through a series of me-
                                                                                 chanical linkages.
                                                                                      The mechanism is essentially a large motor driving a
                 MECHANICAL THRUST DRIVE (DEAN DRIVE)                            flywheel connected to a set of pistons oriented in the three
                                                                                 spatial dimensions. As power is directed to some or all of
                                                                                 the pistons, unidirectional motion (thrust) is created. Inter-
                                                                                 estingly, the predominant skill involved in the operation and
                                                                                 maintenance of thrusters is Mechanical.
                                                                                      Power for thrusters is supplied by the ship’s Power Plant.
                                                                                 The effect of the Thruster is equivalent to Rocket thrust but
                                                                                 without the associated fuel expenditure.
                                                                                      Thrusters do not require reaction mass and operate
                                                                                 independent of gravity. Thrusters can Hover; they operate
                                                                                 without regard to Gravitic or Maneuver Diameter limits.
                                                                                      Inertial Compensation. Because the technology is not
                                                                                 gravity-based, Thrusters do not include inertial compensa-
                                                                                 tion. If required, it must be supplied independently (installing
                                                                                 Lifters is sufficient).

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                              What Jumpspace Looks Like




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                        How Jump Works
                                  The secret of interstellar travel is jump: a process that bypasses spacetime and
                              allows ships to exceed lightspeed.
                    Jump moves a ship from one star system to another in about a week. Start World to Jump Point (the so-called 100D Limit)
                takes about a day (often much lesss), as does travel from the arrival Jump Point to the Destination World. Only if the unusual
                happens need details of jump be considered.

                JUMP DRIVES                                                             tive space where the laws of physics of Real Space do not
                     The key to the stars is the jump drive: an almost magi-            apply. Jump Space is truly alien: it is antagonistic to life and
                cal technology that shifts a ship through a tear in the fabric          in some ways even to matter; time flows differently; distance
                of spacetime into an alternate universe where the generally             has strange relationships with time and energy. Only the
                accepted laws of physics don’t apply.                                   jump field protects a hull and its contents from immediate
                     Jump involves some logical disconnects:                            destruction.

                       • vast amounts of power are required to transition to            Multiple Jump Spaces
                   jump space, but almost no power is required to move                       Jump Space is a continuous series of alternative spac-
                   through it;                                                          es, each with a slightly different set of different physical laws.
                       • the time spent in jump space has little or no rela-            Each of the Jump Spaces is infinite, but smaller (often much
                   tion to the distance travelled; and                                  smaller) than Real Space.
                       • sometimes, jumpspace does not operate in logical                    Jump Space is classified by the distance it tolerates.
                   or predictable ways.                                                 Jump-1 allows a ship to move about a parsec in about a
                                                                                        week. Jump-2 tolerates a ship moving two parsecs in about
                      Jump is possible because of Jump Space: an alterna-               a week. The maximum Jump-9 tolerates nine parsecs.

                 The Vocabulary of Jump
                    A variety of terms have specific meanings in reference to Jump.
                    100 Diameter Limit. 100D Limit. A region centered on a gravity source with a radius equal to 100 times the diameter of
                 the source. A 10,000 kilometer diameter world has a 100D Limit of about a million km.
                    Blockage. The presence of an object (or the 100 D limit of that object) on a courseline. Blockage occurs if the object is
                 larger than the ship in jump.
                    Collateral Damage. Damage inflicted on nearby objects by the energy release of jump.
                    Courseline. An imaginary line connecting the starting point and the ending point of a same jump.
                    Diameter. Twice the radius of a sphere. For a non-spherical object, use its greatest dimension. For a long, narrow object
                 (one dimension is more than five times the sum of the other two dimensions), consider the longest dimension separately.
                    Exit (or Breakout or Precipitation). The act of ending a jump: transitioning from jumpspace to realspace.
                    Failed Jump. An unplanned consequence which results in no jump taking place.
                    Gravity Source. An all-inclusive term for a mass (or an operating gravitic device). The diameter of the source is the dis-
                 tance across its longest dimension (some exceptions apply to very long and narrow objects).
                    Initiation (or Entry). The act of beginning a jump: transitioning from realspace to jumpspace.
                    Jump Mishap. An unplanned consequence of jump resulting in damage to the ship, or to nearby objects.
                    Jump Space. Jump Space is an alternative space in which the laws of physics from the true universe do not apply. Jump
                 Space itself is truly alien: inimical to life and even to matter; it is only the special protections of the jump field that protect a hull
                 and its contents from immediate destruction. Jumpspace exists in multiple levels which are exploited by jump drives to pro-
                 duce different distances travelled. More specific names for portions of jumpspace include hopspace, skipspace, leapspace,
                 boundspace, and vaultspace.
                    Misjump. A consequence of jump resulting in breakout in an unplanned location.
                    Occlusion. The presence of objects which prevent a jump. Occlusion is based on stellar density.
                    Potential. The relative strength of a specific drive, expressed as its output.
                    Real Space. The true universe; opposite of Jump Space.
                    Unplanned Outcomes The general term for a problem with the jump process: Failed Jump. Misjump.
                    Voyage. Any travel process. More specifically, a process through Jump Space (or one of the related spaces).

                can plot a course that intersects a gravity source (a planet,       JUMP BUBBLE
                a star) and the course automatically terminates at the 100D
                limit from the gravity source. This strategy works equally well
                for Hop, Skip, and higher level drives.


                Jump Fields
                    A Jump Drive creates a Jump Field which drags the ship
                tonnage within it into Jumpspace. The Jump Field can be
                expressed as a jump bubble surrounding the ship, or as a
                jump grid closely conforming to the ship’s hull:

                Jump Bubble
                      Without any additional mechanisms, the Jump Drive
                creates a quasi-spherical (often an egg-shaped oblate
                spheroid) bubble. The Naval Architect Manual computes the
                size of the Jump Bubble as:
                      D= (Tons * 13.5 )^1/3 *20 (in meters)
                      For example, a functioning jump drive in a 100 ton ship
                creates a jump bubble with a diameter (=100*13.5 ^1/3 * 20
                = 11.05 *20 =) 220 meters.
                      For example, a functioning jump drive in a 2400 ton ship
                creates a jump bubble with a diameter (=2400 * 13.5 ^1/3
                *20 = 32.3 *20 =) 640 meters.
                      For most purposes, a Jump Bubble is about five times
                the diameter of the average of Length, Width, and Height of         JUMP BUBBLE

                                                                                    D= (Tons * 13.5 )^1/3 *20
                the ship and centered on the Jump Drive.
                      For all practical purposes, the size of a Jump Bubble for
                an Adventure Class Ship is R=4.
                      Cost. The Jump Bubble is inexpensive; it is the default           D= Jump Bubble Diameter (in meters)
                option for jump drives and imposes no additional cost for the           Tons= Hull Tons
                drive or the hull.
                      Advantages. The Jump Bubble imposes the effects of
                the drive on all matter within the bubble. The Jump Bubble is         Jump Bubble for most ACS ships is about R=4.
                well-suited to Cluster and Braced Cluster hulls, or the ships         For ships to 10,000 tons, the Bubble is about R=5.
                which may vary in shape from mission to mission.                      For even the largest of ships (to a million tons or so),
                      Difficulties. Occasionally, a Jump Bubble will enclose        Jump Bubble size is about R=6.
                nearby debris. If the delicate balance of total ship tonnage is
                disrupted, the ship may experience a misjump.
                                                                                    JUMP GRID
                      The Jump Bubble affects each continuous object with-
                in it separately. A jump drive operates on the ship tonnage
                contained within its jump field. The ship with its Jump Drive
                almost always operates properly; other objects within the
                bubble suffer Jump Mishaps.


                Jump Grid
                      A Hull equipped with Jump Grid has a mesh of jumpfield
                conductive wires just below its surface; the Jump Drive effect
                is channeled through these elements to create an efficient
                field which closely conforms to the shape of the hull.
                      Cost. The Jump Grid adds MCr1 per 100 tons of hull (=
                KCr10 per ton of hull).
                      Difficulties. Damaged jump grids are difficult to repair
                (Jump Plates are often bolted over gaps in grid coverage).
                      Jump Grids cannot be effectively installed on Cluster
                and Braced Cluster Hulls.
                      Advantages. The Jump Grid has virtually no chance of
                misjump due to random nearby debris.

                 Unplanned Outcomes
                            Type                       Failed Jump                                             Misjump
                          Cause Drive Fail or                Inside 100D Limit, or        Astrogation           Jump Drive
                                  Power Fail                 Interference with Jump eld   Failure               Failure
                  Time in Jump= 0 Days                       0 Days                       7 Days                7+ Flux Days
                            Exit= No Movement                No Movement                  Blockage/Scatter      As Planned
                           Fuel= Expended                    Expended                     Expended              Expended
                         Mishap in Jump Drive                in Jump Drive                No                    In Jump Drive
                     Time In Jump. Calculated secretly and revealed only when it occurs.
                     Blockage and Scatter are consequences of an Astrogation Fail, calculated at the time the ship exits Jump.
                     Free Trader Cassandra, 200 tons, Jump-1, 1G, is content to fly the fringes and backwaters of the empire; She plans a
                 simple Jump-1 from Hefry to nearby Forboldn. The ship leaves Hefry and flies to about 100 Diameters: Hefry is a Size-2 World
                 about 3,000 km in Diameter. The 100D limit is (3,000 * 100= ) 300,000 km, or about S=6; it takes a couple of hours.
                     Astrogator Bleddyn C+S+K=10 calculates the Astrogation Task (C+S+K=10) on 1D and the result is (1D=4) automatic.
                 With the proper data entered, the ship starts out, spends about 168 hours in JumpSpace, and breaks out at about 100D from
                 Forboldn. This new world is larger: Size-8, about 12,000 km in Diameter. The 100D limit is (12,000 * 100=) 1,200,000 km, or
                 about S=8. It takes about 8 hours to arrive at Forboldn’s starport.
                     Cassandra now moves on to its next destination: Knorbes. After moving to the Jump Point at 100D, Bleddyn calculates
                 the Astrogation Task (C+S+K=) 10 or less on 1D (=6) which is successful. Because the Astrogation Task was successful,
                 there is no blockage or scatter for the Jump.
                     The Jump begins, and after about 168 hours breaks out to Real Space near Knorbes.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
