                 The Mishap
                   Drives (and other mechanisms) may be damaged by the energy of jump, or by exposure to jumpspace.
                   A Mishap occurs during a Failed Jump, or when the Jump Drive fails during Jump, or when a hull fails during Jump.
                   Inflicting Mishap Damage. Mishap damage to a ship begins in the Jump Drive and cascades through the ship. Mishap
                 damage for non-ships is inflicted randomly to the object’s components.
                   Using the ShipSheet, inflict one event of Damage to one SubCompartment (Hit Line) containing Jump Drive. Determine
                 Damage and Diagnosis Severity.
                   Inflict Damage one level less severe (reroll for Diagnosis Severity) on each adjacent SubCompartment (Hit Line).
                   Inflict Damage one level yet less severe (reroll for the Diagnosis Severity) on Hit Lines adjacent to those.
                   Continue the process until the severity of damage is Easy.

                 COURSELINE BLOCKAGE
                     If the Astrogation Task fails, count sixes in the failed roll: The
                 Blockage is at that count in parsecs, short of the destination.
                                                                                                    4    L       I       J      K        L           I
                     Spectacular: pair ones and sixes and determine any excess:

                                                                                                                                O
                     if at least three excess ones, the task is Spectacular Suc-
                 cess, and the arrival is normal.
                     if at least three excess sixes, the task is Spectacular Failure
                                                                                                    5    P M N                           P M
                 and the ship emerges short of the destination.

                 THE     HOP PROCESS
                       Event                                          Crew                  Comment
                    1. Preparation For Hop                                                  Planning
                          Determine Destination System             Captain
                          Astrogation Task Planning              Astrogator
                             Target System                                                  Intended Destination
                             Intervening System                                             Intersect at 1,000 D Limit
                          Astrogation Task Resolution            Astrogator                 Dif culty= Dice = Parsecs
                          Astrogation Task Veri cation           Astrogator                 Dif culty= Dice = Parsecs+1
                          Move to Hop Point (about 10 AU)             Pilot                 1,000 D from all relevant gravity sources.
                    2. Initiate Hop
                          Power Plant Consumes Fuel              Engineer. Power System.
                          Hop Drive Engages                      Engineer. Hop.
                          Hop Entry Flash                                                   Flash Size= Ship Size+4
                             Possible Drive Failure.
                             Possible Hop Field Interference.
                          Transition To Hop Space
                    3. Period in Hop Space                                                  = 168 hours +/- Flux x 2 hours
                          Possible Vector Changes                                           Not possible with Gravitic-based drives.
                    4. End Hop
                          Rumblings                                                         Approximately one hour before Breakout.
                          Hop Exit Flash                                                    Flash Size= Ship Size+ 2
                          Transition to Realspace
                             Hop Drive Fail Effects                                         Time-in-Hop altered.
                             Astrogation Fail Effects
                                  Courseline Blockage            Astrogator                 = net of 6’s minus 1’s on Distance Dice.
                                  Increased Scatter              Astrogator                 = within same subsector.
                                  Overshoot                      Astrogator
                          Normal Scatter                         Astrogator               = Left/Right Flux (max +1/-1). 0 = target system

                 Unplanned Outcomes
                           Type                        Failed Jump                                             Misjump
                          Cause Drive Fail or Power Fail     Interference with Jump eld   Astrogation Failure Jump Drive Failure
                   Time in Hop= 0 Days                       0 Days                       7 Days                7+ Flux Days
                           Exit= No Movement                 No Movement                  Blockage/Scatter      As Planned
                         Mishap in Hop Drive                 in Hop Drive                 No                    In Hop Drive
                     Time In Jump. Calculated secretly and revealed only when it occurs.
                     Blockage and Scatter are consequences of an Astrogation Fail, calculated at the time the ship exits Hop.

                                            Shows Jump Potential based on TL Stage Effects.                          Higher order drives experience TL Stage Ef-
                                            Parenthetic is Drive Potential (Std J-1 has Drive Potential-1).          fects like Jump but at higher Tech Levels.
                                              Jump-0. Jump Drives with Jump Potential less than 1 can achieve Jump exactly equal to potential times 1 parsec
                                            (Jump 0.5 achieves exactly 0.5 parsecs; Jump 0.8 achieves exactly 0.8 parsecs).
                                            From the HSBLV6789 Table
                                              The Hop Drive produces effects at 10^1= 10 times the equivalent Jump Drive.
                                              Compared to the Standard Jump-1 Drive available at TL9, the Standard Hop-1 Drive is available at TL17. The Ad-
                                            vanced H-6 Drive (capable of Hopping 60 parsecs) is available at TL24. Some TL14 world is probably working on an
                                            Experimental Hop Drive, but it doesn’t produce enough potential to actually work.

                                              The Leap Drive produces effects at 10^3= 1,000 times the equivalent potential Jump Drive.
                                              Compared to the Standard Jump-1 Drive available at TL9, the Standard Leap-1 Drive is available at TL22. The Ad-
                                            vanced L-6 Drive (capable of Leaping 6,000 parsecs) is available at TL29.

                                               The 9 Drive produces effects at 10^9= a billion times the equivalent Jump Drive.
                                               Compared to the Standard Jump-1 Drive at TL9, the Standard 9 Drive requires TL34. On the other hand, an Experi-
                                            mental 9-Drive-1 could be produced at TL32. Its performance is a billion parsecs in about a week. A more reliable option
                                            is a Standard 7 Drive-1 at TL32. With a performance of 10^7 (ten million parsecs in about a week), it requires a hundred
                                            “jumps” to the 9 Drive’s one.

                                     Power Systems            COMBINING POWER PLANTS
                                                                   The standard power systems (identified by letters A through Z)
                 Y                                            are the only practical sizes which can be constructed (just as there
                 DRIVE                                        are limitations on the construction of internal combustion engines;
                 COSTS
                 Drive   EP    A        C         U     P     a million-ton version is impossible). Power systems can be ganged
                                                              and their combined output harnessed using Nexi.
                   A     100    31       20       15      4        A Nexus (plural = Nexi) connects the output of two or more (up
                   B     200    32       30       20      7   to 9) identical mechanisms, allowing the joined devices to produce
                   C     300    33       40       25     10   greater overall power output.
                   D     400    34       50       30     13        For example, an N2 or NN P-Plant consists of two N power
                   E     500    35       60       35     16   plants connected by a Nexus. The nexus itself is basically a connec-
                   F     600    36       70       40     19   tion; it adds no tonnage and no additional cost.
                   G     700    37       80       45     22        A Nexus can also be used to create other combinations up to
                   H     800    38       90       50     25   nine drives: N3, K4, Z9. An A9 drive (consisting of nine Standard P-
                   J     900    39      100       55     28   Plant-A drives and nexi) creates an output of 900 EP, is (9 x 4)=) 36
                   K   1000     40      110       60     31   tons, and costs MCr 36. P-Plant-J is more efficient at 28 tons.
                   L    1100    41      120       65     34        Combined drives can themselves be combined (and designated
                   M 1200       42      130       70     37   by a prefix digit): 4A4 indicates ( 4 x 4 = ) 16 P-Plant Drive-A. The
                   N   1300     44      140       75     40   largest drive available under ACS is 9Z9 (= 9 x 9 = ) 81 P-Plant-Z.
                   P   1400     46      150       80     43        The resulting ratings remain subject to TL limitations.
                   Q 1500       48      160       85     46        For example:
                   R   1600     50      170       90     49
                   S   1700     52      180       95     52   Plant                   tons          EP

                  R2 3200      100      340      180     98   other drives to also create greater output.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 DISPERSED POWER SYSTEMS




                 Centralized Or Dispersed
                   A ship power system may have a single source of energy, or the sources may be distributed throughout the ship.
                   Dispersed. The power system supports its interplanetary and interstellar drives. Individual mechanisms are self-powered
                 (with FusionPlus or Power Cells) and independent of a central power source.
                      ● Fusion Plus. Ships may use Fusion Plus (compact cold fusion modules) as the independent power source. Individual
                 components can continue to operate indefinitely (months) without a central power supply.
                      ● Power Cells. Ships may use Power Cells (compact high capacity power storage cells; batteries) as the independent
                 power source. Individual components can operate for (1D) days after disconnection from the central power supply.
                   Centralized. A power system which supplies all power to the operating mechanisms of the ship. Individual mechanisms or
                 areas have short-term emergency power backups (1D hours).

                Fusion Power Plant                                               standing liquid water or deposits of solid water ice on worlds
                     The power plant is a variant of the standard fusion pow-    with Hydrographics 1+.
                er plant redesigned to meet the needs of starship operations.         Unrefined fuel is often available at starports.
                     Fuel. Starship fusion plants fuse molecular hydrogen.            Refined Fuel is available at better quality starports.
                     Raw or unrefined hydrogen fuel (contaminated with hy-            Overclock. Starship Power Plants are “small” genera-
                drogen compounds such as methane CH4, ammonia NH3,               tors compared to the standard plants used on world surfaces.
                water OH2, hydrogen sulfide SH2, and helium) is available        The key to their importance is called Overclock. The Power
                directly from the atmospheres of gas giants. An alternative      Plant can increase its output by several orders of magnitude
                raw or unrefined fuel is water available from bodies of free-    for a period of seconds, with great inefficiency. This ability
                                                                                 even makes normal operation relatively inefficient, with fuel
                 FUSION POWER PLANT                                              usage measured in tons per week rather than tons per year.
                                                                                 In addition, each use of Overclock increases the chance of
                                                                                 failure (which is why starship Power Plants require annual
                                                                                 maintenance).
                                                                                      These Overclock inefficiencies are reflected in the Drive
                                                                                 Tech Level Stage Effects table (in Ship Design) which sig-
                                                                                 nificantly reduces efficiency and increases tonnage for lower
                                                                                 tech levels (and increases efficiency and reduces tonnage
                                                                                 for higher tech levels).

                                                                                 Fission Power Plants
                                                                                      The power plant uses the fission of radioactive elements
                                                                                 to produce usable energy. Its output is similar to Fusion Pow-
                                                                                 er Plants, but it is incapable of Overclock, and cannot sup-
                                                                                 port Interstellar Drive operations.
                                                                                      Fuel. Fission power plants are fueled by radioactives
                                                                                 (typically Uranium, Plutonium, or Thorium). Raw radioactives
                                                                                 are mined as available on worlds or asteroids.
                                                                           - 132 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                    Refined fuel rods are available at some starports (those       FISSION POWER PLANT
                which supply a significant number of ships powered by fis-
                sion power plants). Refined fuel rods can be custom-man-
                ufactured from raw radioactives using a Radioactive Fuel
                Processing Unit, or at TL-5+ manufacturing facilities.

                Anti-Matter Plants
                      The Anti-Matter Plant is a sophisticated Power System
                producing energy from matter-anti-matter encounters.
                      Fuel. Anti-matter fuel slugs contain AM particles with
                magnetic or gravitic fields to isolate them from normal matter.
                The particles can be released slowly as a continuing source
                of energy, or in a burst as required to power starship drives.
                      AM is produced in slugs available at some starports
                (those which supply a significant number of ships powered
                by AM power plants).
                      AM slugs are stored in a 1-ton console near the AM
                Plant. The greatest vulnerability of AM Plants is the poten-
                tial for catastrophic failure: an accidental, environmental, or
                combat hit to the AM fuel supply is sufficient to destroy the     be obtained from any world with Atmosphere 2-9. Refined
                entire ship.                                                      or unrefined status is inconsequential for Fuel Cell fuel. A
                      AM Slug Production. AM Plants are intrinsically capa-       single fuel cell requires one ton of fuel to support 10 weeks
                ble of operating “in reverse” and producing AM Slugs. With        of operations for a Compartment.

                an AM Plant in production mode can produce 1 AM slug per          the operation of its assigned device for ten weeks.
                100 EP per day.                                                        Operations. Fuel Cells, like Power Cells, are suitable

                Collectors                                                        they support Consoles, Computers, and Sensors. They are

                Power Cells
                     The ship has an array of units which store usable en-         ANTI-MATTER POWER PLANT
                ergy. Power cells are dependent on an energy source to pro-
                vide the energy that they store and release.
                     Power cells are batteries: they store energy; they do not
                produce it. An operating Power Plant can charge multiple
                Power Cells in about a day.
                     Routine Energy Use. A charged Power Cell can sup-
                port the operation of its assigned device for two weeks.
                     Operations. Power Cells are suitable for routine use of
                non-energy-intensive devices. In practice, they support Con-
                soles, Computers, and Sensors. They are not suitable for
                Drives, Weapons, or Defenses.

                Fuel Cells
                     The ship has an array of units which convert a source
                fuel into usable energy.
                     Fuel. Fuel cells consume hydrogen and oxygen to pro-
                duce electric current. Hydrogen can be obtained from stan-
                dard starship fuel, or skimmed from gas giants. Oxygen can

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                How Sensors Work
                                 Sensors are the technological eyes and ears (and other senses) of starships
                              (and of many star system installations). Technology magnifies the capabilities of
                              personal senses and translates the information that sensors gather into under-
                              standable formats.

                     The Traveller Sensor System and its mix of devices allows the crew of a starship (or spacecraft, or vehicle, or base, or
                starport, or installation) to acquire knowledge about objects and phenomena around them in a series of information-gathering
                steps. Rather than knowing everything about a star system or plant or region all at once, the information becomes available
                in a series of readings.

                UNDERSTANDING THE TRAVELLER SENSOR SYSTEM                              Sensors are the eyes and ears (or other perception or-
                     The sensor system is based on two principles:                gans). They are operated by specific crew members under
                     The Referee Has Perfect Knowledge. He knows if an            the general job title: Sensor Operator, or Sensop.
                enemy starship is lurking in the planetoid belt, or behind a           The Sensop may be a dedicated, full-time crew mem-
                gas giant. Or, he knows that the present star system is total-    ber, or he may operate the Sensors as part of a greater job
                ly vacant, with no threats or dangers other than natural ones.    responsibility.
                     The Players Have No Knowledge. They initially know
                nothing. They depend on the readings from their sensors to        USING SENSORS
                develop an understanding of the situation.                            Sensor output is provided in three distinct stages, each
                     Dare they risk moving directly to the barren mainworld,      revealing more information.
                not knowing what ships or threats might lie in ambush?
                     Dare they move closer at all, risking being detected by      Alert Stage
                possible enemies as they do?                                          When there is something of possible interest, the Ref-
                     The Sensor system is the process by which the players        eree conveys to the players an Alert:
                learn about potential threats, challenges, and rewards.
                                                                                       “Your [sensor] sees something about [location].
                The Process
                     The Sensor Process takes place as players investigate        Detection Stage
                each new star system, planet, and even planetary location,            Using the Sensor Task, the characters try to resolve
                moving ever closer as they use their sensors, and risking         what gave the alert (or they can ignore it). Success in the
                disaster if they guess (or sense) wrong.                          Sensor Task provides information about the alert.
                     SOARN Sense Only As Really Necessary. The Sen-
                sor Process is a series of steps dealing with the unknown. If     Tracking Stage
                the system is totally unknown to the characters, then using            Once a sensor detects an object, it can track that object
                the Sensor Process is entirely appropriate. If the system is      until some event causes the signal to be lost (it may move
                familiar, then Sensors come into play only when problems or       out of range; or it may be hidden by a world; it may deliber-
                unusual readings occur.                                           ately jam or hide its signal).
                                                                                       A Sensor cannot operate beyond its stated Range.
                UNDERSTANDING SENSORS                                             When Range is exceeded, the contact is lost.
                     Sensors are technological devices which provide in-
                formation about the environment. They are identified by a
                LongName (with enough detail to define its usage) which                                    SENSORS
                includes Stage, Range, Type, and Tech Level. Each specific
                type of Sensor is also identified by a single Letter Code (for
                example, C = Communicator).
                     Types of Sensors. Space Sensors are used over the
                vast distances of space. World Sensors are used over small-
                er distances associated with a world (and are more closely            Sensop / Sensor Operator     Sensors Compartment
                tailored to the information that examination of a world can
                                                                                            Sensors are the eyes and ears of a ship.
                provide).

                IDENTIFYING SENSORS                                               CREATING SENSORS
                    Sensors detect a variety of inputs and process them to            Sensors are defined primarily by Type and Tech Level.
                provide specific details of location, environment, and friends        Sensors are determined during the Ship Design Pro-
                and enemies.                                                      cess, although the details of the chapter help with a better
                    Sensors are identified by a LongName which provides           understanding of the use and capabilities of sensors.
                enough detail to define its usage. A Sensor LongName in-
                cludes Stage, Range, Mount, Type, Tech Level, Mod, Ton-           The Sensor Creation Process
                nage, Cost, Range, and P/A and Principle data).                         For each desired Sensor:
                    Stage is the sensor’s position in the TL spectrum.                  A. Select a Sensor. Note its Model Letter and TL.
                    Range is the sensor’s distance ability. Space Sensors                  Base tonnage for a Sensor is zero tons.
                use Space Ranges. World Sensors use World Ranges.                          Base cost for World Sensor is MCr0.1;
                    Mount. Sensors are contained in a Mount (which is then                 Base cost for a Space Sensor is MCr1.0.
                contained within the identification of the installed sensor).           B. Select a Mount for the Sensor. Mount is TL-indepen-
                    Sensor Name details the precise nature of the mecha-          dent. Note any Mods to the Sensor based on Mount.
                nism and provides insights into how it operates.                           Sensor Mounts (with the exception of Surface and An-
                    Tech Level identifies the Technological Level at which        tenna) are installed in Hardpoints (or Firmpoints).
                the Sensor is commonly manufactured. TL is required.                    C. Apply TL Stage Effects consistent with ship TL.
                    Mod notes the advantage (or disadvantage) the Sensor                D. For Space Sensors, apply Space Range Effects.
                has in the Sensor Task.                                                 E. For World Sensors, apply World Range Effects.
                    Tonnage is the volume of the Sensor and its Mount.                  F. Allocate a Control Panel for each Sensor.
                    Cost is the Sensor acquisition cost.                                G. Note the final cost and tonnage.
                    Range is the specific Range Band in either S= or R=.                   Surface sensor installations do not require hardpoints;
                    P/A Data notes whether the device operates in Passive         added required tons may be located anywhere on the ship.
                Mode, Active Mode, or both.                                             TL Stage Effects apply to the Sensor but not the Mount.
                    Principle shows the physical principle for the device.        It affects cost of the Sensor, but not the tonnage.
                                                                                        Range Effects apply to the Mount but not the Sensor.
                                                                                  A Surface Mount (with no tonnage) can be increased from
                      Early Long Range Bay-Mount Radar-9                          the standard AR S=7 to LR or even DS at no increase in ton-
                      Mod=+4. 100 tons. MCr17. S=09. Active (Elec)                nage and, uniquely for Surface Mounts, at no increased cost
                                                                                  (since the cost multiplier times 0 produces 0).
                    For example, Early Long Range Bay-Mount Radar-9 is                  Multiple Sensors. A ship may install any number of
                an electronic sensor designed to search for objects at Range      sensors, subject only to tonnage restrictions. More than one

                of Radar; its signals are aggressively computer-analyzed               is not effective at mapping internal structure of large bod-
                for detailed information. Passive EMS senses existing EM               ies. It detects operation of various M-Drives, G-Drives, NA-
                radiation (including emissions by the target, reflected local          FAL, Lifters, and inertial compensators, even behind larger
                energy, and occluded background energy). Active EMS proj-              worlds, ships, or objects.
                ects radio pulses in sweeping scans of an area and inter-                   Grav Sensor detects Gravity (Mass) based on Size.
                prets the returned signals (echoes) for information about an                Gravity manipulation produces a Mod + Size.
                objects size, distance, and speed.                                          Related Sensors. The Grav Sensor is an advanced

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 R REMOTE SENSORS
                    Type Z Sensor Missiles (Size 2-3-4-5) can carry one Surface Mount Sensor with Range S= Size (or Range R= Size+5).
                    Type Z Sensor Missiles (Size 6= Small Craft) can each carry one Sensor suitable for a FirmPoint (less than 1.0 tons).
                    Type Z Sensor Missiles (Size 7= Ship) can each carry any suitable Sensors (one HardPoint, or three FirmPoints, or any
                 number of Surface Mounts).
                    Reusable. Standard cost Sensors are reusable; the missile can return to the ship and be redeployed time after time.
                    Disposable. A disposable version of the Sensor suitable for one-time use is one-tenth standard cost.
                    Return-To-Ship Mode. Sensor Missiles can be programmed to return to their launching ship and their gathered information
                 is downloaded.
                    Relay Mode. Sensor Missiles can transmit their gathered information to a DataCaster or a CommCaster installation.
                    The Remote Sensor Task is resolved using C+S= TL.

                 Suitable Sensors (not a complete list)

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                How Weapons Work
                                 Space weapons are produced in many different forms and at many different
                              tech levels, but all have the same goal: to defeat other ships in battles, and to at-
                              tack targets on worlds.
                    The broad array of space weapons reflects the many different ways weapon technology can operate. The interplay be-
                tween weapons and defenses allows for many different weapon use strategies.

                UNDERSTANDING SPACE WEAPONS                                       USING WEAPONS
                      Space weapons have a focused purpose: to damage or              Weapons are used within the Space Combat System,
                destroy enemy spacecraft, or to damage targets on worlds.         which provides a much more complete description of the
                Space Weapons fall into four distinct categories:                 process. In brief, weapon use is an Attack with five steps:

                ticle Accelerators, Plasma and Fusion Guns, Meson Guns,           (or later) Rounds.
                Lasers, Tractors and Pressors, and Disruptors.                         Defenses. An Attack may be deflected, disrupted, or
                      Fields project an area of effect on the target. Stasis,     frustrated by specific defenses. The Attacking Weapon may
                Inducers and Dampers.                                             be disabled.
                      Data Broadcasts or Beamcasts send data at the tar-               Some Defenses (and some Weapons) may operate in
                get. CommCaster and DataCasters.                                  the Defensive Mode, and react to each incoming Attack.

                    UG. UnGuided. No guidance system.
                    HW. Hardwired (=5). Pre-programmed circuits direct missile to target.
                    OG. Operator Guided (=C+S). Gunner directs to the target (must be from S=2 or R=7 or less). Minimum Missile Size=4
                    SA. Self-Aware (=C+S). Missile is controlled by an on-board self-aware Brain. Minimum Missile Size=5
                    DL. Down Loaded (C+S). Missile is controlled by the downloaded personality of the Gunner. Minimum Missile Size=5

                 HOW MANY MISSILES PER TON?
                                                                                        B BATTERIES
                       Missiles are described (in part) by Size, ranging from              All of one Weapon (perhaps with different Mounts) on a
                 Size-1 Bullets to Size-6 Small Craft or Size-7 Ship). Based            single ship may become a Battery: all fire in one Attack.
                 on established Benchmarks, Size-5 is about Human/Person                   TL: The Attack uses DOUBLE lowest TL of the group.
                 Size: more or less 1.5 meters or 5 feet, and by calculation               C+S+K: Skill or Console is ignored.
                 about 72 liters.                                                          Mods: The sum of Mods for all Weapons in the Battery
                       But missiles aren’t liquid, so they can’t be just poured         (including CommCaster).
                 into a missile bay. How many fit in a Ton?                                Hits: The sum of Hits for all Weapons in the Battery (in-
                       Assume a missile is about 1.5 meters long, roughly cy-           cluding CommCaster).
                 lindrical, with a length-width ratio of about 5:1. That makes
                 each a rectangular volume 1.5 x 0.3 x 0.3 meters = 0.135               Virtual Batteries
                 cubic meters each (or 135 liters).                                        CommCaster can create a temporary Virtual Battery of
                       A ton is 13.5 cubic meters: tightly packed, the crew can         the same type of weapon and at the same Range, subject
                 fit about 100 in a shipboard ton.                                      to a one-Round delay: designation in the current Round for
                       Safe storage is probably not that efficient, so assume           execution in the next Round.
                 half or about 50 Size-5 missiles per ton.
                       By extension, Size-4 Missile is about one-eighth the
                 volume (about 10-15 liters), or 400 missiles per ton.
                       Size-3 is one-sixty-fourth the volume (about the size of
                 a 1-liter container), or 3000 missiles per ton.
                       Size-2 is card-sized; Size-1 is bullet-sized. A ton of
                 storage for each is sufficent for all but the most extended
                 battles.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                         How Defenses Work
                              Defenses exist to disrupt or frustrate the actions of weapons.
                   The various defenses reflect different technological approaches to defeat weapons. The interplay between defenses and
                weapons allows for many different weapon use strategies.

                UNDERSTANDING SPACE DEFENSES
                     Space Defenses have a focused purpose: to counter in some way the attacks of weapons (and, to some extent, sensors).
                Space Defenses fall into four distinct categories:
                     Force Fields (Screens) produce or project a barrier to incoming matter and energy. They are further divided into Damp-
                ers, Scramblers, and Globes).
                     Anti-Sensors (created under Sensors) specifically disrupt or confuse sensors as a defense against detection or informa-
                tion gathering.
                     Sandcasters project a burst or cloud of particles that disrupt beams. They operate in the Defense Mode against beams.
                     Some Beam Weapons (created under Weapons) may be used in the Defense Mode against incoming missiles.
                     Some Missile Weapons (created under Weapons) may be used in the Defense Mode against incoming missiles.

                The Process                                                             LongName Elements may be omitted if not applicable.
                     When Weapons attack targets, the target’s Defenses                 Stage (or TL) is the defense’s position in the spectrum of
                operate in Defense Mode. Defenses are created to counter          sophistication in the developmental life cycle. For example,
                Weapon attacks. They are identified by a Defense LongName         Advanced, or Prototype. It amplifies the specific TL of the de-
                (with enough detail to define its usage).                         fense. It is possible for Stage to be blank (primarily if Stage
                     Defenses fall into three types:                              is Standard).
                     World Range Defenses (identified with Range R=),                   Range is the defense’s reach in providing protection. De-
                     World Range Weapons (identified with Range R=) us-           fenses use World Ranges R=. Beam and Missile Weapons
                able in the Defense Mode, and                                     used in Defense Mode have World Range R=. Stealth Mask
                     Anti-Sensors (Jammer and Stealth Mask).                      has a Space Range S=.
                                                                                        Mount states the type of defense mount.
                USING DEFENSES                                                          Defense Name details the precise nature of the mecha-

                Allocating Mounts                                                      Mount Limits. A hull allows one Hardpoint per 100 tons.
                     Each Defense Type has a minimum size for its Mount.         One Mount may be installed at each Hardpoint.
                Select a Mount equal to or greater than the Minimum.                   In some cases (Bays or Mains), the Mount will occupy a
                     Bolt-In Mounts. The primary Defense Mount is the Bolt-      sizable fraction (or even an improper fraction) of the 100 tons
                In: a 3-ton fitting which can be placed anywhere with a ship     associated with its Hard Point.
                and does not require a HardPoint or FirmPoint, nor access              FirmPoints. A hull may allocate, instead of HardPoints,
                to the exterior of the hull.                                     three FirmPoints per 100 tons. A Firmpoint will accept any
                     Other mounts (turrets, barbettes, and others) have ad-      mount which is less than one ton (a full-ton mount does not
                vantages and are sometimes used.                                 fit on a FirmPoint).

                The Defense Creation Process
                    For each desired Defense:                                           Standard Vdistant Bolt-In Nuclear Damper-14
                    A. Select a Defense. Note its Model Letter and TL.                  Mod=+3. 3 tons. MCr4. R=07
                       Base tonnage for a Defense is zero tons.
                    B. Select a Mount for the Defense. Mount is TL-inde-               In effect, any Weapon TL can be arbitrarily increased
                pendent. Note any Mods to the Defense based on Mount.             with no changes to costs, tonnage, or mods. The only change
                    C. Apply TL Stage Effects consistent with ship TL.            is TL and its participation in the Weapons Task.
                    D. Space Range Effects are rarely used for Defenses.
                    E. For Defenses, apply World Range Effects. Defense                                 DEFENSE CREW
                Range can be decreased but not increased.
                    F. Allocate a Control Panel each Defense.
                    G. Note the final cost and tonnage.

                How Fuel Works
                                 Spacecraft (indeed, spacecraft and other vehicles) require fuel in the course of
                              their operations.
                    The different types of power plants for starships and spacecraft depend on distinct types of fuel, each with its own benefits
                and complications. The common types of fuel are: Hydrogen, Radioactives, Anti-Matter, and Exotic Particles.

                HYDROGEN                                                              refined fuel, a Power Plant runs the risk of failure for each
                     The standard fuel for starships with Fusion Power Plants         additional week of use: Check (2D) TL (Mod minus weeks of
                is Hydrogen, usually stored as Liquid Hydrogen.                       use). Failure produces failure of the Power Plant.
                     Units. Liquid Hydrogen is tallied in tons, a unit of volume           FusionPlus modules (alternatively called Fusion+, Fu-
                of about 13.5 cubic meters, and not coincidentally, about one         sion Plus, Cold Fusion) are small, sealed, self-contained
                thousand kilograms or one metric ton.                                 cold fusion power sources. Virtually maintenance free, they
                     Characteristics. Liquid Hydrogen is a colorless liquid           are sited throughout many ships as independent power
                with Temperature -11 (about 20 Kelvin) and a viscosity (re-           sources. Powered by water, they are inspected and cleaned
                sistance to flow) about one percent that of water.                    annually, at which time their small water tank is refilled.
                     Availability. Hydrogen is easily acquired. Naturally oc-              Rocket Fuel. Rockets (including HEPlaR) are fueled
                curing gaseous hydrogen (contaminated by various com-                 by Liquid Hydrogen and Liquid Oxygen burning to produce
                pounds) is “raw” and, while usable as fuel, benefits from             thrust. Any electrolysis process (for water or ice) which pro-
                purification to ideal standards. Liquid water (pumped from            duces Liquid Hydrogen also produces Liquid Oxygen for
                lakes or rivers), and water ice (harvested from ice caps,             about the same price. Where Rockets or HEPlaR are in gen-
                planetoids, and comets) can be electrolyzed to produce gas-           eral use, Starports have them available at Cr500 per ton.
                eous hydrogen (and useful life support oxygen). Then cooled
                and liquefied, it is stored in a ship’s tanks until required.         RADIOACTIVES

1
                              BRIEF COMBAT SYNOPSIS
                                 Target Detection uses the Sensor Task based on Sensor TL, C+S+K, and several Mods, and resolved
                              with Range Dice.
                                 After an Attack is declared, but before it is resolved, the Target may defend with Defensive Fire.
                                 Weapon Attack is based on Weapon TL, C+S+K, Target Size, and several Mods, and resolved with
                              Range Dice.
                                 Missile Attack from R=6+ or S=6+ is instead based on Missile TL + Guidance + Missile Size and
                              resolved with 5D
                                 Penetration is Based on Mount Hits. Roll Dice= Hits and apply against Armor and Protection.
                                 Damage is automatic after Penetration: one or more SubCompartment (and crew) Out-Of-Action.
                                 Immediate Action (by Damage Control) can restore an Out-Of-Action SubCompartment. Check per
                              Round.
                                 First Aid can restore an Out-Of-Action crew member. Roll per Combat Round.

                                 Massive Explosions from Missiles occur outside of Armor and must Penetrate Armor and Protection.
                                 Massive Explosion from Anti-Matter on-board a ship occurs inside Armor and Protections: it com-
                              pletely destroys a full Compartment.
                                 EMP which Penetrates makes all Computers and Consoles InOp for two Rounds.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                    How Space Combat Works
                       Conflict in space, between starships and spacecraft, is resolved using Adventure
                      Class Ships and the Traveller Space Combat System.
                       Space Combat resolves conflict between the full range of spacecraft, starships, and small craft based on the weapons
                and defenses used, the decisions of the commanders involved, and some measure of chance. Combat is based on variable
                scales which give the feel of authenticity without slavish adherence to exact formulas. Distance is a coarse set of approximate
                ranges. Time is a coarse measure of passing time. Size is an approximation of the relative size of objects and targets.
                       Finally, the Space Combat system assumes that many attacks and many shots are taking place, but a significant portion
                of them go wild and come to nothing: the misses are unimportant; the hits are what count. The system also assumes that there
                are lulls in the action in which characters wait or think or catch their collective breaths. Space combat accomplishes all of these
                realistic elements without burdening the players with arbitrary or constraining rules.
                       The Traveller Space Combat System resolves conflict between Adventure Class Ships: starships created using the ACS
                Design System.
                       Adventure Class Ships ACS range in size from 100 tons to 2400 tons and operate singly or in small units (squadrons,
                flotillas, task forces) of several ships each. The mix of available ships includes both starships and spacecraft, and both ships
                and small craft. Adventure Class Ships are suitable for use by Traveller role-playing characters, and Space Combat includes
                opportunities for individual characters to make decisions and take actions that influence, guide, or determine outcomes.
                       Battle Class Ships BCS are larger than 2400 tons and are created using the BCS Design System. BCS ships operate
                in fleets and squadrons and include some small ships only where necessary. Battle Class Ships represent major efforts by
                powerful navies responding to strategic, political, and diplomatic imperatives. The role of individual characters in such opera-
                tions is slight. Although BCS is mentioned for completeness, it is not provided in any detail here.
                       Fleet Class Ships FCS are strategic and economic constructs suitable for abstract interstellar interactions. As with BCS,
                FCS is mentioned for completeness, but not provided in any detail here.

                THE ELEMENTS OF A FIGHT
                   Space Combat includes the following elements:                    The Battle
                                                                                         The ships attack, defend, move, and otherwise act in a
                The Situation                                                       series of Combat Rounds, or simply Rounds.
                     The situation is an encounter: any encounter which may              In each Round, every ship has the opportunity to attack
                escalate to space combat. Both sides have goals and the             other ships, to defend against enemy attacks, and to move.
                situation already dictates that violence may possibly be used            The activities in a Round have no specific order: a Par-
                to resolve the conflict; the time for negotiation is past. The      ticipant or a Ship (that is, the Player responsible) states his
                encounter is defined by:                                            intention and starts the process to resolve it:

                        The Participants. Participants are players and                  Free Trader Ithaka attacks that Yacht,
                     non-player characters. Players are the crew of ships;
                     their opposition consists of non-player characters                   Every Participant has the opportunity (but need not take
                     controlled by the referee.                                     it) to Attack until all of its available weapons have fired, and
                        The Ships. The specific ships are pre-defined star-         every Participant has the opportunity to Move or not.
                     ships or spacecraft created using the ACS Adventure                  Interrupts. In some situations, another Participant may
                     Class Ship design system. Each ship requires a com-            interrupt (is allowed to interrupt) in order to respond, or to act
                     pleted ShipSheet with the data required for resolving          first because of a higher priority.
                     combat.                                                              Attacks. Space Combat Attacks take a specific se-
                        The Location. The situation defines the place               quence in order to promote some bit of order to the situation.
                     where the battle will occur: usually a star system and         Once an Attack begins, it follows a defined series of actions.
                     its worlds as they apply to the encounter. Location                  Non-Standard Activity. In addition to generally accept-
                     also defines local bases and local forces which may            ed Space Combat activities (Attacks, Defenses, Movement),
                     participate or interfere in the battle.                        Particpants can incorporate any other appropriate activities

                    With Particpants, Ships, and Location in place, the             essary for other reasons): Sensor Use, Communications

                The Search                                                                Combat Rounds continue until one side is defeated or
                     The Participants may not know each other’s location:           has fled the battlefield, or simply until Combat is no longer

                     Sensor use allows each side to try to structure the situ-      The Aftermath

                A VEHICLE FOR ROLE-PLAYING                                          ATTACKS
                     The Traveller Space Combat System is intended as a                An Attack has five steps: Declared, Defenses, To Hit,
                detailed process in support of role-playing. Individual char-       Penetration, and Damage.
                acters can, and should, be assigned specific responsibilities
                aboard a ship and participate in making decisions and taking        Declared Attack
                the actions necessary to implement them.                                 A Weapon is declared as Attacking a specific Target
                                                                                    (and optionally a Location on the Target). Declaring an At-
                SHIP CARDS                                                          tack begins the Space Combat Sequence.
                     Each Participant ship or small craft requires a Ship Card      Defenses
                detailing its structure and components.                                  The Target of an Attack may state a Defense, which
                     It serves two purposes:                                        is resolved before the Attack can continue. If a Defense is
                     The Weapon/Defense/Sensor List shows the compo-                successful, the Attack is stopped (or sometimes reduced).

                                                                                    - 200 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                              Space Combat

                                                                                                                   Change                             1
                         Ships can change Range Bands in the course of combat.
                         Agility is Power Plant Potential minus Current used Gs, or
                       Maximum Gs minus Current used Gs. In the event of Con ict
                       on who can Move, a ship with greater Agility moves last.
                                                                                                                    Range             11
                       Movement Sequence
                         Ships move in a descending sequence based on tonnage (larger ships move before smaller ships). Ships which are
                       Clustered may move together.
                         Ramming. After all Range Changes, a ship or craft at Boarding Range may Ram. A ship at Boarding Range with an
                       enemy ship may declare Ramming. The target may Check Double Skill Pilot (2D) to avoid the collision. Each receives Hits
                       equal to opponent’s [Number of] Compartments Squared at Location= Flux
                         Boarding. After all Range Changes, a ship or craft at Boarding Range may declare Boarding.
                         Launching and Docking. After all Range Changes, Ramming, and Boarding, any ship or craft which is Docked may
                       Launch. Any ship or craft at Boarding Range may Dock. Ships and Craft may not Dock and Launch in the same Round.
                         Jump. A ship can escape a battle by Jumping (assuming it has Fuel and is beyond the 100 D Limit).


                       Space Ranges                        Time (in Rounds or suf xed h or d) to Change Range Band at Constant G

                              1000 m        Boarding                   1       1         1     1       1       1       1       1       1
                              Surface  0     Contact                                          Target Ship                                           0
                       G= Current Acceleration in G’s.

                       INTERFERENCE
                         Ships (including Small Craft) can Cluster together for protection; those nearby are protected by each other’s Defenses
                       which are within Range.
                         Protection. A Ship, Fighter, or Small Craft within Defensive Range of another Ship, Fighter, or Small Craft is (or can be)
                       protected by the Defenses of those other ships.
                         Interference (variously Fighter Interference, or Ship Interference). A Ship, Fighter, or Small Craft within R=7 of a
                       Target being Attacked can Interfere with that Attack:
                         Against Missile or Beam, it may use AM or AB Fire.
                         Against other Attacks, it may use an Available Defense.
                         If all else fails, it may substitute itself as the Target of the Attack.
                         Decoy Interference. A Decoy Missile within R=7 of the Target of an Attack may substitute itself as the Target of an At-
                       tack. The Decoy Missile must equal the Target Size minus 1 or larger.

                         LASOB. Launched At Start Of Battle. Assumes preplanned attacks. Resolved in Turn number equal to S.
                         RINR. Resolve In Next Round. If red in the current Turn, the Attack is resolved in the Next Turn.
                         Delay. If red in the current Turn, the Attack is resolved in S turns later. 1 Day. Attacks launched 1 day prior to battle.
                         No. No Attack is possible.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 RAMMING
                       A ship or small craft which moves to Boarding Range
                                                                                                                                                 1
                 with a Target may declare Ramming. The Target Check
                 Double Skill Pilot (2D) to avoid the collision. Ramming in-
                 flicts Hits=[Number of] Compartments squared to the Tar-
                 get; roll flux for the location. Simultaneously, the Attacking
                 ship receives Hits= Target Ship [Number of] Compartments
                 Squared; roll Flux for the location.


                 INTERFERENCE
                       A ship or small craft which moves to Boarding Range
                 with a Target may declare Ramming. The Target Check
                 Double Skill Pilot (2D) to avoid the collision. Ramming in-
                 flicts Hits=[Number of] Compartments squared to the Tar-
                 get; roll flux for the location. Simultaneously, the Attacking
                 ship receives Hits= Target Ship [Number of] Compartments
                 Squared; roll Flux for the location.

                    UG. UnGuided. No guidance system.
                    HW. Hardwired (=5). Pre-programmed circuits direct missile to target.
                    OG. Operator Guided (=C+S). Gunner directs to the target (must be from S=2 or R=7 or less). Minimum Missile Size=4
                    SA. Self-Aware (=C+S). Missile is controlled by an on-board self-aware Brain. Minimum Missile Size=5
                    DL. Down Loaded (C+S). Missile is controlled by the downloaded personality of the Gunner. Minimum Missile Size=5

1                Quick Generation
                                                                               Directly create the Trade Classi cations for a world without
                                                                               creating the UWP (if the situation will not otherwise use the
                UWP). The UWP can be created later if necessary. Pick one of the two tables and roll 4D for the row followed by 1D for the
                column. The result is the reasonable, random Trade Classi cations for the world.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                      Trade and Commerce Between The Stars
                                  Interstellar trade is the transport of commercial goods from one world to anoth-
                              er in the pursuit of profit. Successful traders obey the ancient First Law of Trade:
                              Buy Low And Sell High

                     Merchants who follow it make money, grow rich, and become successful; those who don’t go bankrupt.
                     The Traveller Trade and Commerce System details a consistent process for encountering and pricing trade goods, and
                for determining the market for goods on a variety of worlds.

                THE VOCABULARY OF INTERSTELLAR TRADE                                who follow it make money, grow rich and become successful.

                TYPES OF INTERSTELLAR TRADE GOODS                                 businesses or organizations which can use it. For example,
                     Interstellar trade goods may be of any type, but some        tax records may indicate likely customers for specific goods;
                are more probable than others. Ordinary materials (perhaps        reports might provide clues (after analysis) for prediction of
                iron ingots) are probably not prime interstellar trade goods.     future tax revenues, economic trends, or commercial activity.
                     The 14 Broad Types of Goods: Interstellar Trade                    Samples. Newly discovered, created, or manufactured
                Goods fall into 14 distinct types.                                items may be shipped to other worlds for analysis, research,
                     Consumables. Consumables are food and drink, and             or evaluation.
                may also include aromatics. Consumable foods are fashion-               Scrap/Waste. The trash of some worlds can become
                able gourmet goods (caviar), common flavorings (spices),          the valued treasure of others.
                or staples (basic life-sustaining food) necessary on worlds             Uniques. Some products are unique: they cannot be
                where it cannot be produced economically. Consumable              easily synthesized or reproduced. An exotic wood that adds
                drinks are flavored waters, alcoholic beverages, milks, nec-      interest as a decoration or flavor as when burned for cook-
                tars, syrups, decoctions such as teas, or exotic wines. Con-      ing; an herb which provides a special flavoring; an irides-
                sumable aromatics are smell sources or food enhancers.            cent feather which becomes fashionable for a limited time; a
                     Data. Data is Information which can be consumed, re-         pebble that makes gentle noises when heated.
                produced, or processed on the Market World: Books, tapes,               Valuta. Sometimes shipments between worlds consist
                software, creative works, wafers, and scientific data.            of money itself. Interstellar trade eventually produces an in-
                     Entertainments. Creative works and diversions are al-        equity in the balance of payments for specific worlds, and
                ways in demand.                                                   to bring the economy back into equilibrium, a physical ex-
                     Imbalances. If a trade item’s production cost is very        change of money is required.
                low, it can be shipped and sold at a market for less than it
                costs to produce locally. Worlds with low labor costs often
                produce very cheap goods for interstellar markets.

1 THE TRADE GOODS CHARTS
                     The random trade goods creation system can produce 36 different goods per basic trade classification, and thousands
                of distinct trade goods before it consistently repeats. This list is 264 examples (out of thousands) of probable trade goods.

                 Accountings             Bulk Oxygen               Expert Systems           Luminescents              Reparables
                 Adhesives               Bulk Particulates         Famous Wafers            Mag Emitters              Replicating Clays
                 Aged Meats              Bulk Pelts                Fast Drug                Magnetics                 Repulsant
                 Allotropes              Bulk Petros               Fauna                    Mandates                  Respirators
                 Alloys                  Bulk Pharma               Fermented Fluids         Masterpieces              Restoratives
                 Anagathics              Bulk Precipitates         Filter Mask              Meat Delicacies           Robots
                 ANIFX Blocker           Bulk Protein              Fine Aromatics           Mechanicals               Secretions
                 ANIFX Dyes              Bulk Spices               Fine Art                 Meson Barriers            Seedstock
                 ANIFX Emitters          Bulk Synthetics           Fine Carpets             Metals                    Self-Defenders
                 Antibiotics             Bulk Textiles             Fine Dusts               Minerals                  Self-Solving Puzzles
                 Antidotes               Bulk Woods                Fine Furs                Money Cards               ShimmerCloth
                 Antifungals             Candies                   Fission Suppressant      Monumental Art            Silanes
                 Anti-Matter             Carbons                   Flavored Air             Motile Plants             Silver
                 Antique Art             Catalysts                 Flavored Drinks          Museum Items              Skin Tones
                 Antiques                Chelates                  Flavored Waters          Music                     Slow Drug
                 Antiseptics             Coinage                   Flavorings               Musical Instruments       Sludges
                 Antitoxins              Cold Light Blocks         Flill                    Navigators                Software
                 Antivirals              Cold Sleep Pills          Flora                    Nectars                   Soothants
                 Archeologicals          Cold Welders              Flowers                  Noisemakers               Sophont Cuisine
                 Armor                   Collectible Books         Fluidic Timepieces       Non-Fossil Carcasses      Sophont Hats
                 Aromatics               Collectibles              Fluidics                 Nostrums                  Soundmakers
                 Art                     Combat Drug               Foodstuffs               Novel Flavorings          Sparx

                      Generic trade activity has no need for specific trade
                good identifications. Trade goods are identified for the ben-
                efit of adventurers: for the curious merchant character who
                asks “What’s in that container?” checks the manifests, and
                perhaps even breaks a seal and looks inside.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                  Technology
                                 Tools are used to adapt, change, or control an environment. Tool-using cultures
                              are groups of beings (not necessarily sophonts) with the ability to find or make
                              tools. But, using tools is not enough:

                                 Technology is the ability to use tools to make other tools.
                                 Only when societies make the leap to using tools to make tools do they become
                              technological. Technology builds on the successes of earlier tool-making experi-
                              ences, and enables a progression to ever-higher levels.
                     There are three speci c values to technology.
                     Technology is a labor multiplier. A laborer with technology can produce more than a similar person unassisted.
                     Technology enhances quality. Technological output is more consistent and has higher quality.
                     Technology can achieve impossibilities. A person using technology can create objects or results which are impos-
                sible without the use of technology.
                DEFINING TOOLS                                                    There Are Alternatives To Technology
                     Tools are the objects and processes that sophonts use             There are also non-technological activities which can
                to manipulate the universe. A hammer is a tool for construc-      achieve the results of technology. Social groups with poor

                                                                                  sophonts with clumsy manipulators) may develop alternative
                Tools Shape Their Users                                           or non-technological cultures.
                     Technological societies benefit from the tools they use,          Cultures. Some societies use Culture (the behavioral

                USING TECHNOLOGY                                                     CALCULATE BASE TL
                      Worlds (societies, cultures, civilizations, stellar federa-            Stage      TL             Base TL=
                tions, interstellar empires) are classified by their available         Exp Experimental -3               +3
                technology. Each is evaluated on the available technology              Pro Prototype    -2               +2
                and assigned a Technology Level (or Tech Level, or TL).                Ear Early        -1               +1
                      Technology Level indicates the common capabilities of             Std Standard     0                0
                the world in the creation of and use of technology.                    Bas Basic         0                0
                      The data entry for a world may state that it is TL-10. The         Alt Alternate   0                0
                Visitors can expect to find TL-10 equipment in common use              Imp Improved     +1               -1
                in society; they can expect that local repair shops can fix TL-        Gen Generic      +1               -1
                10 equipment; they can expect they can find TL-10 devices             Mod Modi ed       +2               -2
                for sale.                                                              Adv Advanced     +3               -3
                      Common TL Excludes Other TLs. The local TL identi-                Ult Ultimate    +4               -4

                  Experimental


                TECH LEVEL STAGES
                   Tech Level Stages describe locations or situations within the long term cycle of technological development. For example,
                   Experimental is handmade from exciting new technology, usually one-of-a-kind, and often dangerous and unreliable.
                   Prototype is the first step before early mass production. There are only dozens or examples of any one prototype.
                   Early is the first mass-produced design, before the technology has been completely refined.
                   Standard is the version with the expected features for the technology when it is mature and stable.
                   Basic is a cheaper, bulkier, less-featured version of the standard item.
                   Alternate is a rethinking of the application of technology, often emphasizing different results or outputs.
                   Improved is the implementation of additional features.
                   Modified is a specialized version created in response to specific needs.
                   Generic is equivalent to Standard but produced at lower cost using higher tech level manufacturing capabilities.
                   Advanced has significant added capabilities beyond Improved.
                   Ultimate implements significant improvements learned over the life cycle of the product. Post Ultimate is new technology.

                   Understanding Tech Level Stages. Analyze a common type of device (a car, a rifle, an entertainment system, a com-
                municator) by assigning it TL Stages and visualize precisely what each Stage means.




                 TECH LEVEL STAGE EFFECTS
                    TL Stage            TL Cost Mod        Q R E B S Comments
                  Exp Experimental -3 x10           -3     Q - 2 - 3 +3 - 3 One of a kind. Lesser capabilities. Heavier. High cost.
                  Pro Prototype         -2     x5   -2     Q - 2 - 2 +2 - 2 Rare. Lesser capabilities. Heavier and more costly.
                  Ear Early             -1     x2   -1     Q     - 1 +1 - 1 Primitive. Heavy. Costly.
                   Std Standard          0     x1    0     Q                   Typical of available models.
                  Bas Basic              0     /2    0     Q     - 1 +1        Heavier, but cheaper, than Standard.
                    Alt Alternate        0     x1    0     Q F F F F Different capabilities.
                  Imp Improved          +1     x1   +1     Q +1 +1 -1 +1 Some improved capabilities. Greater Ease of Use.
                  Gen Generic           +1     /2    0     5 0 0 0 0 Cheaper than (but near identical to) Standard.
                  Mod Modi ed           +2     /2   +2     Q +2 +2 -2 +2 Includes changed to postively affect performance.
                  Adv Advanced          +3     x2   +3     Q +3 +3 - 3 +3 Lighter, added capabilities. More costly.
                   Ult Ultimate         +4     x3   +4     Q +4 +4 - 4 +4 Lighter, most effective. More costly.
                 F= Flux (the value may vary depending on the manufacturer). Q= Quality = 2D-2.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                THERE IS A MAXIMUM
                     Every Tech Level is built on the advances of the previous ones, and ultimately, tool-making tools surpass the capabilities
                of the sophonts who use them. Computers surpass sophonts in intelligence, and more importantly, in sophont-like insights.
                Computer interfaces raise the abilities of sophonts to new levels and blur the distinction between tool and user.
                     Geneering and medical science increase the intelligence and talents of sophonts to a degree that accelerates technologi-
                cal advance even more.
                     Technological knowledge increases exponentially. Each Tech Level represents an expanding body of knowledge:
                many of the technological discoveries themselves increase the rate at which technology advances.
                     Technological progress accelerates exponentially. The time between technological levels decreases, assuming a
                large population working on technology and sufficient resources being devoted to it, and assuming the cultures involved care
                about advancing technology.
                     As a society reaches beyond TL-21 and approaches TL-Z, technology provides everything, in high quality, on demand, to
                everyone in the society. Robots and sophontoids do all the work; artificial intelligences manage all of society’s systems; indi-
                viduals can choose any number of entertainments or challenges, access most of the knowledge of the universe, do anything,
                experience everything, and all without risk, and strangely enough, without challenge or a feeling of reward (which recalls the
                importance of the Third Law Of Trade).
                     The Technological Singularity. The endpoint in the TL scale achieves tools that are self-replicating, self-improving, and
                panscient.

                                                                      TL-Z =
                                                            The Technological Singularity=
                                                                         TL-33
                    At the Technological Singularity, all things are possible: tools^2 respond to all needs with no discernible delay.
                    The Technological Singularity is Unstable. The features and the abilities of the Technological Singularity promote any
                number of consequences, all of which lead to changes that end the Technological Singularity. At the singularity, sophonts must
                grow or die. Various societies choose various options, but in every circumstance, they must choose:

                      Some consequences are:
                      Society moves beyond technology advancing to metaphysical pursuits which transcend a physical existence.
                      Society retreats to a simple, non-technological pastoral existence.
                      Society collapses, losing its high tech tools and beginning the cycle of technological development again.
                      Society fragments; some factions transcend; others retreat; still others struggle to maintain or reacquire the Singularity.

                  Z The Technological Singularity
                   Levels above 21 are the Accelerating Tech Levels: each is of short duration and each leads at an ever-accelerating pace
                to the next. The technologies addressed by TLs above 21 are frighteningly powerful and exceptionally susceptible to disaster.
                   Which Way? As a society progresses to each new Accelerating Tech Level, Flux determines the focus technology column
                for the Level (although the other technology also becomes available). When the society reaches TL-Z, the sum of the Points
                for that technology is a DM on a single Societal Flux for Destiny.

                 The Singularity

                                                                                   Plane

1 FT Fantastic Technology
                                  Technology

                                                                                                               Technology beyond TL-21 borders on
                                                                                                          or achieves the fantastic: it is not routinely
                available to lower tech levels, and it harnesses capabilities only dreamed of by lesser technologies.
                     Fantastic technology provides fantastic challenges as well. Each individual society faces decisions on the use of tech-
                nology: for good or ill, to build or to destroy, to conquer or to uplift; to share or to squander. Those challenges, more than the
                technology itself, shape the individual societies.
                     Achieving These Technologies. The fantastic technologies shown here are possible: their achievement by a society
                depends on the specific goals and needs of that society.
